metadata.title: Diva: Dynamic Range Filter for Var-Length Keys and Queries
metadata.titleTranslation: Diva：面向变长 Key 和 Query 的动态范围过滤器

sections.title: ABSTRACT
sections.originalMarkdown:
Range filters are compact probabilistic data structures that answer approximate range emptiness queries. They are used in many domains, e.g., in key-value stores, to quickly rule out the existence of keys in a given query range and avoid having to search for them in storage. However, all existing range filters exhibit at least one of the following shortcomings: (1) they do not provide robust false positive rate and performance guarantees, (2) they do not support variable-length keys and query ranges, and (3) they do not allow dynamic operations such as insertions, deletions, or expansions.

We introduce Diva, the first range filter to address all the above challenges simultaneously. Diva learns the dataset’s distribution by sampling keys and storing them in a cache-efficient trie. It compresses the keys in-between samples by removing their longest common prefix and truncating their suffixes while leaving enough bits in the middle (i.e., an infix) to allow differentiating between the keys in the sorted order. It stores infixes in constant time dynamic data blocks, which it splits to handle insertions and expansions. It processes a range query by traversing the trie and checking for the inclusion of infixes in the target query range.

We show, theoretically and empirically, that Diva achieves a false positive rate on par with the state of the art on real-world datasets while supporting dynamicity and variable-length queries and keys.
sections.translationMarkdown:
Range filter 是一种紧凑的概率型数据结构，用于回答近似的范围空性查询。它被用于许多领域，例如在 key-value store 中快速排除给定查询范围内存在 key 的可能性，从而避免在存储中搜索这些 key。然而，现有的所有 range filter 至少存在以下一种缺点：(1) 无法提供稳健的假阳性率与性能保证；(2) 不支持变长 key 和变长查询范围；(3) 不允许插入、删除或扩容等动态操作。

我们提出 Diva，这是首个同时解决上述所有挑战的 range filter。Diva 通过对 key 进行采样并将样本存储在 cache-efficient trie 中来学习数据集的分布。它删除样本之间各 key 的最长公共前缀，并截断其后缀，同时在中间保留足够的 bit（即 infix），以便按照排序顺序区分这些 key，从而压缩样本之间的 key。它将 infix 存储在支持常数时间操作的动态数据块中，并通过拆分数据块来处理插入和扩容。它通过遍历 trie，并检查目标查询范围内是否包含 infix 来处理范围查询。

我们从理论和实验两方面证明，在支持动态性以及变长查询和变长 key 的同时，Diva 在真实世界数据集上实现了与当前最先进方法相当的假阳性率。

sections.title: 1 INTRODUCTION
sections.originalMarkdown:
**What is a Filter?** A filter is a memory-efficient probabilistic data structure that answers whether a query key exists in a given set. Its compactness allows it to fit in a higher level of the memory hierarchy than the set it represents, making it fast to query. A filter never returns a false negative but may return a false positive with a probability called the False Positive Rate (FPR) that depends on its memory footprint. Due to these qualities, filters are used in many applications to avoid redundant disk reads [21] and network hops [10] when a query happens to target a nonexistent key.

**Range Filters and Applications.** Traditional filters answer membership queries for a single key. In contrast, a range filter accepts two keys as the end-points of a range and determines if at least one key in the set is in-between them. As with a traditional filter, a range filter does not return false negatives but may return false positives. Range filters are used in many domains, especially in the context of LSM-Trees [38], to avoid accessing files that do not contain any keys within a range predicate, significantly boosting performance [2, 12, 23, 29, 33, 37, 46, 51]. They also aid in preventing redundant I/Os to SQL tables [2] and B-Tree indices [23], and they have potential applications in social web analytics [16] and replication in distributed key-value stores [44].

**Range Filtering Goals.** The ideal general-purpose range filter must simultaneously support (G1) the lowest possible FPR under a stringent memory budget, (G2) range queries of any length, (G3) variable-length keys, (G4) dynamic modification operations such as insertions, deletions, expansions, and contractions, and the best possible (G5) query and (G6) construction performance.

**Design Contentions.** Every existing range filter only fulfills a subset of these goals [2, 12, 17, 23, 24, 29, 33, 37, 46, 47, 51]. In fact, almost none of the existing ones attain (G3) or (G4) [12, 17, 24, 29, 33, 37, 46, 47]. Moreover, Goswami et. al. have proven an information-theoretic lower bound on the memory footprint of range filters [27], stating that it is impossible for any range filter to achieve (G1) and (G2) at the same time. However, this lower bound only holds for worst-case datasets, meaning that it may still be possible to achieve (G1) and (G2) for “common” datasets. As such, we pose the following research question: Is it possible to design a range filter that simultaneously fulfills all six goals for common datasets? This paper presents an affirmative answer.

**Core Contribution: Diva.** We introduce Diva, the first range filter to support dynamic operations, variable-length queries and keys, and high performance, all at the same time. Diva learns the dataset’s distribution by sampling keys and storing them in a cache-efficient trie. For all keys in-between two samples of the trie, Diva removes the longest common prefix. It also truncates their suffixes while keeping enough bits in the middle of each key (i.e., an infix) to differentiate them in most cases, thus achieving (G1). At the same time, the trie separates dense and sparse regions of the key space. This allows for handling short range queries over densely populated regions and long range queries over sparse regions, thus meeting (G2). By discretizing all keys into fixed-length infixes without the use of hashing, Diva achieves (G3). This discretization also allows for storing the infixes of adjacent groups of keys within a constant time data structure called an Infix Store, fulfilling (G5). As Diva derives infixes without hashing and stores them in the original sorted order of the keys, it does a single efficient sequential pass over the dataset during construction, making it the fastest range filter to construct. It therefore attains (G6). Diva handles dynamic updates by splitting Infix Stores, thereby meeting (G4).

**Additional Contributions.**

- We quantify the necessary conditions on the data distribution for Diva to achieve a low FPR and memory footprint while supporting variable-length range queries.
- We mathematically prove Diva’s low FPR and high performance properties.
- We empirically evaluate Diva against other range filters in static and dynamic settings. We also conduct end-to-end experiments on top of WiredTiger [36], a popular B-Tree-based key-value store.
sections.translationMarkdown:
**什么是 Filter？** Filter 是一种内存高效的概率型数据结构，用于回答查询 key 是否存在于给定集合中。由于它十分紧凑，因此与其所表示的集合相比，可以放置在内存层次结构中更高的一层，从而能够快速查询。Filter 从不返回假阴性，但可能以某一概率返回假阳性；该概率称为假阳性率（False Positive Rate，FPR），取决于 filter 的内存占用。由于这些性质，当查询恰好指向一个不存在的 key 时，filter 在许多应用中被用于避免冗余磁盘读取 [21] 和网络跳转 [10]。

**Range Filter 及其应用。** 传统 filter 回答单个 key 的成员查询。相比之下，range filter 接受两个 key 作为一个范围的端点，并判断集合中是否至少有一个 key 位于二者之间。与传统 filter 一样，range filter 不会返回假阴性，但可能返回假阳性。Range filter 被用于许多领域，尤其是在 LSM-Tree [38] 中，它可以避免访问不包含范围谓词内任何 key 的文件，从而显著提高性能 [2, 12, 23, 29, 33, 37, 46, 51]。它们还可帮助避免对 SQL 表 [2] 和 B-Tree 索引 [23] 的冗余 I/O，并可能应用于社交网络分析 [16] 以及分布式 key-value store 的复制 [44]。

**Range Filtering 的目标。** 理想的通用 range filter 必须同时支持：(G1) 在严格的内存预算下实现尽可能低的 FPR；(G2) 任意长度的范围查询；(G3) 变长 key；(G4) 插入、删除、扩容和缩容等动态修改操作；以及尽可能好的 (G5) 查询性能与 (G6) 构建性能。

**设计上的矛盾。** 每一种现有 range filter 都只能满足这些目标的一个子集 [2, 12, 17, 23, 24, 29, 33, 37, 46, 47, 51]。事实上，几乎没有现有方法能够达到 (G3) 或 (G4) [12, 17, 24, 29, 33, 37, 46, 47]。此外，Goswami 等人证明了 range filter 内存占用的信息论下界 [27]，指出任何 range filter 都不可能同时实现 (G1) 和 (G2)。然而，该下界仅对最坏情况数据集成立，这意味着对于“常见”数据集，仍可能同时实现 (G1) 和 (G2)。因此，我们提出如下研究问题：能否设计一种 range filter，使其针对常见数据集同时满足全部六个目标？本文给出了肯定答案。

**核心贡献：Diva。** 我们提出 Diva，这是首个同时支持动态操作、变长查询、变长 key 和高性能的 range filter。Diva 通过采样 key 并将样本存储到 cache-efficient trie 中来学习数据集的分布。对于 trie 中两个样本之间的所有 key，Diva 删除它们的最长公共前缀。它还截断其后缀，同时在每个 key 的中间保留足够多的 bit（即 infix），使其在大多数情况下可以彼此区分，从而实现 (G1)。与此同时，trie 将 key 空间划分为稠密区域和稀疏区域。这使 Diva 能够处理稠密区域上的短范围查询以及稀疏区域上的长范围查询，从而满足 (G2)。Diva 不使用 hashing，而是将所有 key 离散化为定长 infix，从而实现 (G3)。这种离散化还允许把相邻 key 组的 infix 存储在一种称为 Infix Store、支持常数时间操作的数据结构中，从而满足 (G5)。由于 Diva 无需 hashing 即可导出 infix，并按照 key 的原始排序顺序存储它们，因此在构建期间只需对数据集进行一次高效的顺序扫描，使其成为构建速度最快的 range filter，从而实现 (G6)。Diva 通过拆分 Infix Store 来处理动态更新，从而满足 (G4)。

**其他贡献。**

- 我们量化了 Diva 在支持变长范围查询的同时实现低 FPR 和低内存占用所需的数据分布条件。
- 我们从数学上证明了 Diva 的低 FPR 和高性能性质。
- 我们在静态和动态场景中对 Diva 与其他 range filter 进行了实证比较。我们还基于流行的 B-Tree key-value store WiredTiger [36] 开展了端到端实验。

sections.title: 2 PROBLEM ANALYSIS
sections.originalMarkdown:
This section shows that no current range filter satisfies all of the six goals outlined in Section 1. In-depth summaries of these filters are presented in [23, 42].

**Range Filtering Definitions.** A range filter represents a set $S$ of keys coming from a universe of size $u$. Given a range query of the form $q = [q_l, q_r]$, the filter checks if the range is empty by answering whether $q \cap S = \emptyset$ with an FPR of at most $\epsilon$, where $0 < \epsilon < 1$. The top three rows of Table 1 summarize terms describing the range filtering problem throughout the paper.

**Table 1: Definitions of terms and symbols.**

| Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|
| $q = [q_l, q_r]$ | An inclusive query range. |
| $\epsilon$ | Target FPR, i.e., probability of a false positive. |
| $L$ | Average key length. |
| $(a)_2$ | Binary value represented by $a$. |
| $T$ | Number of keys between two samples. |
| $m_i^{shared}$ | Length of the longest common prefix of the $i$-th and $(i + 1)$-th samples, in bits. |
| $m^{infix}$ | Length of the infixes. |
| $m_i^{redundant}$ | Number of redundant bits corresponding to the $i$-th and $(i + 1)$-th samples. |
| $\alpha$ | Load factor of the Infix Stores. |
| $x_q$ | Quotient of the infix $x$. |
| $x_r$ | Remainder of the infix $x$. |

**Memory Lower Bound.** A range filter is Robust if it guarantees an FPR of at most $\epsilon$ for any dataset. A non-robust range filter often drops all information about the keys’ lower-order bits. This can lead to a high FPR when range queries predicate over these lower-order bits. It is known that any robust range filter supporting queries of length up to $R$ must use at least $\log_2 \frac{R}{\epsilon} - O(1)$ Bits per Key (BPK) [27], meaning that answering longer range queries requires more memory. Intuitively, this is because a robust range filter supporting longer queries must carry more information about which areas of the key space are empty.

**Robustly Achieving (G1), (G2), and (G3) is Impossible.** In most applications, filters are allotted a stringent memory budget of 8-16 BPK to fit in memory (with a higher budget, one may as well store the full keys). If we rearrange the above lower bound in terms of range query length $R$ and plug in 16 BPK as the memory budget and $\epsilon = 0.01$ as the target FPR, we find that a robust range filter can only answer range queries of length at most 512. Such short query lengths limit the applicability of the filter.

The lower bound also implies that a robust range filter cannot support variable-length keys. With variable-length keys, there can be infinitely many possible keys within a range, meaning $R = \infty$. Plugging $R = \infty$ into the lower bound, we find that infinite memory is needed to support variable-length keys. In sum, it is impossible to attain all of (G1), (G2), and (G3) with a robust range filter.

Indeed, none of the existing robust range filters [17, 23, 33] support variable-length queries (G2) or keys (G3), as they assume range query lengths bounded by $R$. Rosetta [33] stores prefixes of keys based on length in a hierarchy of Bloom filters of depth $\lceil \log_2 R \rceil$, with lower levels storing longer prefixes. Grafite [17] encodes $N$ integer keys in a bitmap of size $N\epsilon R$ by mapping each to a bit using a locality-preserving hash function and setting it to 1. Memento filter [23] splits each key into a prefix and a $\lceil \log_2 R \rceil$-bit suffix, and it hashes each prefix to compute a fingerprint key to a compact hash table and stores its corresponding suffix in it. In general, issuing longer range queries forces each of these filters to issue more Bloom filter probes (in Rosetta), check more bits (in Grafite), or check more fingerprints (in Memento filter). If the range query length exceeds the intended value of $R$ used to construct these filters, the FPR grows beyond $\epsilon$ and approaches 1.

**Semi-Robust FPR Guarantee.** This paper observes that, despite the aforementioned impossibility, one can achieve (G1), (G2), and (G3) by providing a semi-robust FPR guarantee: Any query must be answered with an FPR of at most $\epsilon$ when the dataset comes from a “well-behaved distribution.” Intuitively, a well-behaved distribution is one for which the cumulative distribution function is smooth. This property is commonly satisfied in practice since input datasets typically follow well-known, smooth distributions (e.g., Uniform, Normal, Zipfian, Power Law, Poisson). We formally define well-behaved distributions and show that Diva provides the above FPR guarantee for them in Section 4.

Interestingly, the learning-augmented filters [8, 31] SNARF [46] and Oasis+ [12] fulfill (G1) and (G2) with the same semi-robust FPR guarantee. They do so by fitting a linear spline model to the keys’ CDF, using the model to map each key to a bit in a large bitmap, and setting the bit to 1. These filters handle variable-length queries by leveraging the monotonicity of this mapping and searching the bits corresponding to the query for a 1. There is no formalism or proof of the above FPR guarantee in their respective publications, though one could adapt Diva’s proof in Section 4 to them.

Nevertheless, SNARF and Oasis+ do not meet (G3), (G4), and (G5). They assume fixed-length keys when learning the distribution, not attaining (G3). Although SNARF provides insertion and deletion APIs, they are prohibitively slow. This is because SNARF (and Oasis+) encode their bitmaps using the Elias-Fano scheme [22, 25] and partition them into blocks of $B$ entries to save space and speed up decoding. SNARF’s insertion and deletion APIs are slow since they rewrite an entire block and change its size, potentially increasing it to $O(N)$ over time. Oasis+ disallows dynamic operations since it prunes away empty regions of the key space to improve its FPR. Thus, both do not achieve (G4). They are also slow to query due to using binary search, thereby not meeting (G5).

**Challenges of Attaining Dynamicity (G4).** Range filters exhibit an intrinsic contention between support for fast dynamic operations and memory efficiency. Many existing range filters optimize for memory by operating as Bloom filters in their core, hashing keys into a bitmap and setting bits from 0s to 1s [24, 29, 33, 37, 47]. As with standard Bloom filters, such filters cannot support deletes (by resetting a bit back to 0) or expansions (by remapping the 1s to a larger bitmap) without introducing false negatives [23].

Other range filters utilize compact encoding schemes (e.g., succinct tries [51] or Elias-Fano [12, 17, 46]). Such formats are difficult to update, as they tightly pack their data to avoid storing pointers and offsets. This necessitates changing their entire representation in memory to make room for new insertions.

As of today, Memento filter [23] is the only filter that supports insertions and deletions in expected constant time. However, since it doubles its size to expand, it wastes as much as 50% of its capacity. Diva overcomes this contention between memory efficiency and dynamicity by establishing a one-to-one mapping between keys and the metadata it stores to facilitate deletions, similarly to Memento filter and other expandable filters [5, 18, 19, 23]. Furthermore, It slightly overprovisions memory to absorb insertions and avoids wasting memory by expanding in small increments.

**Contention between Supporting Variable-Length Queries (G2) and Query Speed (G5).** It is an open question whether achieving fast operations while supporting variable-length range queries is possible. Grafite [17] and Memento filter [23] are the only filters that provide constant time queries. They do so by localizing partitions of size $R$ (i.e., the maximum range query length) of the key space to the same memory region. It is unclear how to achieve a similar localization with variable-length range queries. As such, Grafite and Memento filter do not fulfill (G2). All other filters that attain (G1) and (G2), i.e., SNARF [46] and Oasis+ [12], use predecessor search to handle queries, which requires super-constant time [6, 7]. In practice, these filters use binary search to compute predecessors. Diva alleviates this contention by employing a $y$-Fast trie [48], leading to faster predecessor searches.

**Construction Speed (G6).** Existing range filters extensively use hashing [17, 23, 24, 29, 33, 37, 47], floating point operations [12, 46], or trie traversal [29, 51] during construction. Such operations lead to high CPU and cache miss costs. In contrast, Diva makes limited use of hashing, avoids floating point operations, and has good cache locality, attaining the best construction speed and (G6).

**Other Range Filters.** Proteus [29] employs a SuRF [51] instance with a tunable height and a Bloom filter storing key prefixes of a tunable length. It co-tunes these structures based on a sample of the query workload. While it performs well when the workload remains the same, it does not bound the FPR in the face of workload shifts. As it also inherits the limitations of SuRF and Bloom filters discussed earlier, it does not address any of the goals.

REncoder [24, 47] and bloomRF [37] are variants of Rosetta [33] that improve its speed by co-locating bits representing similar prefixes in a single bitmap. However, in doing so, they assign the same FPR to all levels in the hierarchy, breaking the robust FPR guarantee. Since they inherit Rosetta’s other limitations, they do not attain the other goals either.

**Summary.** Table 2 summarizes the goals each range filter attains, and Table 3 outlines their operation costs and memory footprint. They demonstrate that no range filter achieves all of (G1), (G2), (G3), and (G4). They also highlight the question of how fast a range filter can be while meeting these goals, i.e., (G5) and (G6).

**Table 2: Diva is the first range filter to simultaneously support variable-length queries and keys, as well as dynamicity.**

| Filter | Robust FPR (G1) | Semi-Robust FPR (G1) | Var.-Len. Queries (G2) | Var.-Len. Keys (G3) | Dynamic (G4) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| SuRF |  |  | ✓ | ✓ |  |
| Rosetta | ✓ | ✓ |  |  |  |
| REncoder |  |  |  |  |  |
| bloomRF |  |  |  |  |  |
| Proteus |  |  |  |  |  |
| SNARF |  | ✓ | ✓ |  |  |
| Oasis+ |  | ✓ | ✓ |  |  |
| Grafite | ✓ | ✓ |  |  |  |
| Memento | ✓ | ✓ |  |  | ✓ |
| Diva |  | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |

**Table 3: We compare the range filters’ time and space complexities assuming an FPR of $\epsilon$, query length $R$, and $N$ keys of average length $L$. For SuRF and Proteus, $z$ is the number of internal nodes in the trie, while $m$ denotes the length of the fingerprints stored at the leaves. For REncoder, $k$ is its number of hash functions, which is roughly an $O(\log \frac{1}{\epsilon})$ value. For SNARF, $B$ is the block size. For Memento and Diva, $\alpha$ is the load factor. The operation costs are measured as the expected number of cache misses. The construction analysis assumes that the filters are constructed on sorted keys. The range query columns focus on empty and non-empty range queries, respectively. The filters marked with * do not provide mathematical bounds on their memory consumption. Therefore, following [23], we give a conservative estimate of their memory footprint to enable a comparison.**

| Filter | Construction | Insert | Delete | Range Query (-) | Range Query (+) | BPK |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SuRF [51] | $O(NL)$ | - | - | $O(L)$ | $O(L)$ | $10 + 10z/N + m + o(1)$ |
| Rosetta [33] | $O(N\log(R/\epsilon))$ | $O(\log(R/\epsilon))$ | - | $O(\log R)$ | $O(\log(R/\epsilon))$ | $1.44\cdot\log_2(R/\epsilon)$ |
| REncoder [24, 47] * | $O(Nk)$ | $k$ | - | $k$ | $k$ | $O(k+\log(1/\epsilon))$ |
| bloomRF [37] * | $O(N(L-\log N))$ | $O(L-\log N)$ | - | $O(L-\log N)$ | $O(L-\log N)$ | $\approx 1.2\cdot\log_2(R/\epsilon)$ |
| Proteus [29] | $O(N(L+\log(1/\epsilon)))$ | - | - | $O(L)$ | $O(L+\log(1/\epsilon))$ | $10z/N+1.44\cdot\log_2(1/\epsilon)$ |
| SNARF [46] | $O(N)$ | $O(\log N+B)$ | $O(\log N+B)$ | $O(\log N)$ | $O(\log N)$ | $2.4+\log_2(1/\epsilon)$ |
| Oasis+ [12] | $O(N)$ | - | - | $O(\log N)$ | $O(\log N)$ | $\approx 2.4+\log_2(1/\epsilon)$ |
| Grafite [17] | $O(N\log N)$ | - | - | $3$ | $3$ | $2+\log_2(R/\epsilon)+o(1)$ |
| Memento [23] | $O(N)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $1-4$ | $1-4$ | $\frac{1}{\alpha}(3.125+\log_2(R/\epsilon))$ |
| Diva [Static] | $O(N)$ | - | - | $O(\log L)$ | $O(\log L)$ | $3.19+\log_2(1/\epsilon)$ |
| Diva [Dynamic] | $O(N)$ | $O(\log L)$ | $O(\log L)$ | $O(\log L)$ | $O(\log L)$ | $1.19+\frac{1}{\alpha^2}(2+\log_2(1/\epsilon))$ |
sections.translationMarkdown:
本节说明，目前没有任何 range filter 能满足第 1 节所列出的全部六项目标。[23, 42] 对这些 filter 给出了深入总结。

**Range Filtering 定义。** Range filter 表示来自大小为 $u$ 的全集的 key 集合 $S$。给定形如 $q=[q_l,q_r]$ 的范围查询，filter 通过回答 $q\cap S=\emptyset$ 是否成立来检查该范围是否为空，其 FPR 至多为 $\epsilon$，其中 $0<\epsilon<1$。表 1 的前三行总结了全文中用于描述 range filtering 问题的术语。

**表 1：术语和符号的定义。**

| 符号 | 定义 |
|---|---|
| $q=[q_l,q_r]$ | 闭区间查询范围。 |
| $\epsilon$ | 目标 FPR，即发生假阳性的概率。 |
| $L$ | 平均 key 长度。 |
| $(a)_2$ | $a$ 表示的二进制值。 |
| $T$ | 两个样本之间的 key 数量。 |
| $m_i^{shared}$ | 第 $i$ 个和第 $(i+1)$ 个样本的最长公共前缀长度，以 bit 为单位。 |
| $m^{infix}$ | infix 的长度。 |
| $m_i^{redundant}$ | 与第 $i$ 个和第 $(i+1)$ 个样本对应的冗余 bit 数。 |
| $\alpha$ | Infix Store 的负载因子。 |
| $x_q$ | infix $x$ 的 quotient。 |
| $x_r$ | infix $x$ 的 remainder。 |

**内存下界。** 如果一个 range filter 能够针对任意数据集保证 FPR 不超过 $\epsilon$，则称其为 Robust。非稳健 range filter 通常会丢弃 key 低位 bit 的全部信息。当范围查询的谓词涉及这些低位 bit 时，这会导致很高的 FPR。已知任何支持最长为 $R$ 的查询的稳健 range filter 都必须使用至少 $\log_2\frac{R}{\epsilon}-O(1)$ Bits per Key（BPK）[27]，这意味着回答更长的范围查询需要更多内存。直观地说，这是因为支持更长查询的稳健 range filter 必须携带更多关于 key 空间中哪些区域为空的信息。

**无法以稳健方式同时实现 (G1)、(G2) 和 (G3)。** 在大多数应用中，为使 filter 能够驻留内存，只会为其分配严格的 8–16 BPK 内存预算（预算再高就不如直接存储完整 key）。如果将上述下界按照范围查询长度 $R$ 重新整理，并代入 16 BPK 的内存预算和目标 FPR $\epsilon=0.01$，会发现稳健 range filter 最多只能回答长度为 512 的范围查询。如此短的查询长度限制了 filter 的适用性。

该下界还意味着稳健 range filter 无法支持变长 key。对于变长 key，一个范围内可能存在无限多个可能的 key，即 $R=\infty$。将 $R=\infty$ 代入下界可知，支持变长 key 需要无限内存。总之，稳健 range filter 不可能同时达到 (G1)、(G2) 和 (G3)。

事实上，现有稳健 range filter [17, 23, 33] 都不支持变长查询 (G2) 或变长 key (G3)，因为它们假定范围查询长度以 $R$ 为上界。Rosetta [33] 根据长度将 key 前缀存储在深度为 $\lceil\log_2R\rceil$ 的 Bloom filter 层次结构中，较低层存储较长前缀。Grafite [17] 使用 locality-preserving hash function 将每个整数 key 映射到一个 bit 并将其置 1，从而把 $N$ 个整数 key 编码到大小为 $N\epsilon R$ 的 bitmap 中。Memento filter [23] 将每个 key 拆分为一个前缀和一个 $\lceil\log_2R\rceil$-bit 后缀，对每个前缀进行 hash，以计算紧凑 hash table 中的 fingerprint key，并在其中存储对应后缀。总体而言，发出更长的范围查询会迫使这些 filter 分别执行更多 Bloom filter probe（Rosetta）、检查更多 bit（Grafite）或检查更多 fingerprint（Memento filter）。如果范围查询长度超过构建这些 filter 时采用的预期 $R$，FPR 就会超过 $\epsilon$ 并趋近于 1。

**半稳健 FPR 保证。** 本文观察到，尽管存在上述不可能性，但可以通过提供半稳健 FPR 保证来实现 (G1)、(G2) 和 (G3)：当数据集来自“良态分布”时，任何查询的 FPR 都必须不超过 $\epsilon$。直观而言，良态分布是累积分布函数平滑的分布。实践中通常满足这一性质，因为输入数据集通常遵循一些已知的平滑分布（例如 Uniform、Normal、Zipfian、Power Law 和 Poisson）。我们将在第 4 节形式化定义良态分布，并证明 Diva 针对这些分布提供上述 FPR 保证。

有趣的是，learning-augmented filter [8, 31] SNARF [46] 和 Oasis+ [12] 也通过同样的半稳健 FPR 保证满足 (G1) 和 (G2)。它们对 key 的 CDF 拟合 linear spline model，使用该模型将每个 key 映射到一个大型 bitmap 中的一个 bit，并将该 bit 置 1。它们利用这种映射的单调性处理变长查询，搜索与查询对应的 bit 中是否存在 1。其论文并未形式化或证明上述 FPR 保证，但可以将 Diva 在第 4 节的证明改造后用于它们。

然而，SNARF 和 Oasis+ 不满足 (G3)、(G4) 和 (G5)。它们在学习分布时假定 key 定长，因而未达到 (G3)。尽管 SNARF 提供插入和删除 API，但速度慢到难以接受。这是因为 SNARF（以及 Oasis+）使用 Elias-Fano 编码 [22, 25] 编码 bitmap，并将其划分为每块 $B$ 个 entry 的 block，以节省空间并加速解码。SNARF 的插入和删除 API 很慢，因为它们会重写整个 block 并改变其大小，随着时间推移，block 大小可能增至 $O(N)$。Oasis+ 不允许动态操作，因为它会剪除 key 空间中的空区域以改善 FPR。因此，两者都无法实现 (G4)。此外，由于使用 binary search，它们的查询速度也很慢，因而不满足 (G5)。

**实现动态性 (G4) 的挑战。** Range filter 在支持快速动态操作与内存效率之间存在内在矛盾。许多现有 range filter 以 Bloom filter 为核心工作机制来优化内存：将 key hash 到 bitmap 中，并把 bit 从 0 置为 1 [24, 29, 33, 37, 47]。与标准 Bloom filter 一样，如果不引入假阴性，这些 filter 就无法支持删除（把 bit 重置为 0）或扩容（把 1 重新映射到更大的 bitmap）[23]。

其他 range filter 使用紧凑编码方案（例如 succinct trie [51] 或 Elias-Fano [12, 17, 46]）。这类格式为了避免存储指针和 offset 而紧密打包数据，因此很难更新。为了给新插入腾出空间，必须改变其在内存中的整个表示。

截至目前，Memento filter [23] 是唯一支持期望常数时间插入和删除的 filter。然而，由于扩容时将大小翻倍，它会浪费多达 50% 的容量。与 Memento filter 和其他 expandable filter [5, 18, 19, 23] 类似，Diva 在 key 与为支持删除而存储的 metadata 之间建立一一映射，从而克服内存效率与动态性之间的矛盾。此外，它稍微超额配置内存以吸收插入，并通过小幅增量扩容来避免浪费内存。

**支持变长查询 (G2) 与查询速度 (G5) 之间的矛盾。** 能否在支持变长范围查询的同时实现快速操作仍是一个开放问题。Grafite [17] 和 Memento filter [23] 是仅有的两种提供常数时间查询的 filter。它们将 key 空间中大小为 $R$（即最大范围查询长度）的分区局部化到同一内存区域。如何针对变长范围查询实现类似局部化尚不明确。因此，Grafite 和 Memento filter 不满足 (G2)。其他所有同时达到 (G1) 和 (G2) 的 filter，即 SNARF [46] 和 Oasis+ [12]，都使用 predecessor search 处理查询，而这种搜索需要超常数时间 [6, 7]。实践中，这些 filter 使用 binary search 计算 predecessor。Diva 采用 $y$-Fast trie [48] 缓解了这一矛盾，从而实现更快的 predecessor search。

**构建速度 (G6)。** 现有 range filter 在构建期间大量使用 hashing [17, 23, 24, 29, 33, 37, 47]、浮点运算 [12, 46] 或 trie 遍历 [29, 51]。这些操作会造成很高的 CPU 与 cache miss 开销。相比之下，Diva 仅有限使用 hashing，避免浮点运算，并具有良好的 cache locality，因此实现最佳构建速度并达到 (G6)。

**其他 Range Filter。** Proteus [29] 使用一个高度可调的 SuRF [51] 实例，以及一个存储可调长度 key 前缀的 Bloom filter。它根据查询 workload 的样本协同调优这些结构。虽然 workload 保持不变时表现良好，但 workload 变化时无法限制 FPR。由于它还继承了前述 SuRF 和 Bloom filter 的局限，因此没有解决任何一项目标。

REncoder [24, 47] 和 bloomRF [37] 是 Rosetta [33] 的变体，它们通过把表示相似前缀的 bit 共同放置在同一个 bitmap 中来提高速度。然而，这会给层次结构中的所有层分配相同 FPR，破坏稳健 FPR 保证。由于它们继承了 Rosetta 的其他局限，也无法达到其他目标。

**总结。** 表 2 总结了每种 range filter 所达到的目标，表 3 列出了其操作成本和内存占用。二者表明，没有任何 range filter 能同时实现 (G1)、(G2)、(G3) 和 (G4)。它们还突出了这样一个问题：在满足这些目标的同时，range filter 能有多快，即能否实现 (G5) 和 (G6)。

**表 2：Diva 是首个同时支持变长查询、变长 key 和动态性的 range filter。**

| Filter | 稳健 FPR (G1) | 半稳健 FPR (G1) | 变长查询 (G2) | 变长 key (G3) | 动态 (G4) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| SuRF |  |  | ✓ | ✓ |  |
| Rosetta | ✓ | ✓ |  |  |  |
| REncoder |  |  |  |  |  |
| bloomRF |  |  |  |  |  |
| Proteus |  |  |  |  |  |
| SNARF |  | ✓ | ✓ |  |  |
| Oasis+ |  | ✓ | ✓ |  |  |
| Grafite | ✓ | ✓ |  |  |  |
| Memento | ✓ | ✓ |  |  | ✓ |
| Diva |  | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |

**表 3：假设 FPR 为 $\epsilon$、查询长度为 $R$，且有 $N$ 个平均长度为 $L$ 的 key，我们比较各 range filter 的时间和空间复杂度。对于 SuRF 和 Proteus，$z$ 是 trie 中内部节点的数量，$m$ 表示叶节点所存 fingerprint 的长度。对于 REncoder，$k$ 是其 hash function 数量，大致为 $O(\log\frac{1}{\epsilon})$。对于 SNARF，$B$ 是 block 大小。对于 Memento 和 Diva，$\alpha$ 是负载因子。操作成本以期望 cache miss 次数衡量。构建分析假定在已排序 key 上构建 filter。范围查询两列分别关注空范围查询和非空范围查询。标有 * 的 filter 未给出其内存消耗的数学界。因此，遵循 [23]，我们给出保守的内存占用估计，以便进行比较。**

| Filter | 构建 | 插入 | 删除 | 范围查询 (-) | 范围查询 (+) | BPK |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SuRF [51] | $O(NL)$ | - | - | $O(L)$ | $O(L)$ | $10 + 10z/N + m + o(1)$ |
| Rosetta [33] | $O(N\log(R/\epsilon))$ | $O(\log(R/\epsilon))$ | - | $O(\log R)$ | $O(\log(R/\epsilon))$ | $1.44\cdot\log_2(R/\epsilon)$ |
| REncoder [24, 47] * | $O(Nk)$ | $k$ | - | $k$ | $k$ | $O(k+\log(1/\epsilon))$ |
| bloomRF [37] * | $O(N(L-\log N))$ | $O(L-\log N)$ | - | $O(L-\log N)$ | $O(L-\log N)$ | $\approx 1.2\cdot\log_2(R/\epsilon)$ |
| Proteus [29] | $O(N(L+\log(1/\epsilon)))$ | - | - | $O(L)$ | $O(L+\log(1/\epsilon))$ | $10z/N+1.44\cdot\log_2(1/\epsilon)$ |
| SNARF [46] | $O(N)$ | $O(\log N+B)$ | $O(\log N+B)$ | $O(\log N)$ | $O(\log N)$ | $2.4+\log_2(1/\epsilon)$ |
| Oasis+ [12] | $O(N)$ | - | - | $O(\log N)$ | $O(\log N)$ | $\approx 2.4+\log_2(1/\epsilon)$ |
| Grafite [17] | $O(N\log N)$ | - | - | $3$ | $3$ | $2+\log_2(R/\epsilon)+o(1)$ |
| Memento [23] | $O(N)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $1-4$ | $1-4$ | $\frac{1}{\alpha}(3.125+\log_2(R/\epsilon))$ |
| Diva [Static] | $O(N)$ | - | - | $O(\log L)$ | $O(\log L)$ | $3.19+\log_2(1/\epsilon)$ |
| Diva [Dynamic] | $O(N)$ | $O(\log L)$ | $O(\log L)$ | $O(\log L)$ | $O(\log L)$ | $1.19+\frac{1}{\alpha^2}(2+\log_2(1/\epsilon))$ |

sections.title: 3 DIVA
sections.originalMarkdown:
We present Diva: the first range filter to simultaneously support (G1) a low FPR and memory consumption, (G2) arbitrarily sized range queries, (G3) variable-length keys, (G4) dynamic updates, deletes, and resizability, and high (G5) query and (G6) construction performance. Diva picks every $T$-th key in the ordered key set as samples and stores them in a cache-efficient trie to approximate the key distribution. It inserts all keys between two samples into a compact data structure called an Infix Store, as shown in Figure 1. For all keys in each Infix Store, Diva removes the longest common prefix. It also removes the variation in the keys’ length by truncating a suffix from each while leaving enough bits to differentiate the keys and guarantee a given FPR. This FPR guarantee holds when the dataset follows a “well-behaved” distribution (as proven in Section 4). Figure 2 illustrates the truncation and mapping of keys into an Infix Store.

Diva processes a range query by searching for the query’s endpoints in its trie. A range query intersecting at least one sample in the trie immediately returns a positive since that sample represents a key within the range. In contrast, a range query that falls between two adjacent samples searches the corresponding Infix Store and returns a positive if at least one overlapping key exists. For ease of exposition, we first describe a static version of Diva in this section. We generalize it to dynamic key sets in Section 3.5. Table 1 summarizes the terms used to describe Diva.

**Figure 1: Diva learns the dataset’s distribution by storing every $T$-th key as a sample in a trie. It manages all keys between two adjacent samples in an Infix Store.**

**Figure 2: For all keys in-between a pair of adjacent samples in the trie, Diva removes the longest common prefix and truncates the longest possible suffix while still ensuring that there are enough bits left to distinguish between the keys.**
sections.translationMarkdown:
我们提出 Diva：首个同时支持以下目标的 range filter：(G1) 低 FPR 和低内存消耗；(G2) 任意大小的范围查询；(G3) 变长 key；(G4) 动态更新、删除与大小调整；以及较高的 (G5) 查询性能和 (G6) 构建性能。Diva 从有序 key 集合中每隔 $T$ 个 key 选取一个样本，并将其存入 cache-efficient trie，以近似 key 分布。它将两个样本之间的所有 key 插入一种称为 Infix Store 的紧凑数据结构，如图 1 所示。对于每个 Infix Store 中的所有 key，Diva 删除其最长公共前缀。它还截断每个 key 的后缀，从而消除 key 长度的差异，同时留下足够多的 bit 来区分 key 并保证给定 FPR。当数据集遵循“良态”分布时，该 FPR 保证成立（证明见第 4 节）。图 2 展示了 key 的截断及其到 Infix Store 的映射。

Diva 通过在 trie 中搜索查询端点来处理范围查询。如果范围查询与 trie 中至少一个样本相交，则立即返回 positive，因为该样本代表范围内的一个 key。相反，如果范围查询落在两个相邻样本之间，Diva 就搜索相应的 Infix Store；若存在至少一个重叠 key，则返回 positive。为便于阐述，本节先介绍 Diva 的静态版本。第 3.5 节将其推广到动态 key 集合。表 1 总结了用于描述 Diva 的术语。

**图 1：Diva 将每第 $T$ 个 key 作为样本存入 trie，以学习数据集分布。它用一个 Infix Store 管理两个相邻样本之间的所有 key。**

**图 2：对于 trie 中一对相邻样本之间的所有 key，Diva 删除最长公共前缀，并在仍能确保留下足够 bit 区分这些 key 的前提下截断尽可能长的后缀。**

sections.title: 3.1 Sampling Keys and Deriving Infixes
sections.titleTranslation: 3.1 采样 Key 与导出 Infix
sections.originalMarkdown:
The static variant of Diva requires the input to arrive in sorted order. This requirement is commonly satisfied in practice. For instance, many database tables and indices are structured as sorted column files [3, 4, 13, 26, 45], B-Trees [14, 43], or LSM-Trees [15, 20, 21]. If the input is unsorted, users must sort it before construction.

Diva samples every $T$-th key from the ordered key set and inserts them into its trie. The trie approximates the distribution of the keys. The reason is that adjacent samples lexicographically close to each other correspond to denser regions of the key space, while faraway samples correspond to sparser regions. Thus, the trie “learns” the data distribution. The smaller $T$ is, the more accurate the approximate distribution becomes, yet the larger the trie and the memory footprint grow. We quantify the relationship between $T$ and the approximation accuracy in Section 4. We have empirically found that $T = 1024$ provides good all-round accuracy and performance with little memory overhead ($\approx 1\%$ of the total memory).

**Removing the Longest Common Prefix.** Consider a pair of consecutive trie samples. We refer to them as the Predecessor and Successor of the keys between them. Figure 1 shows an example of predecessor and successor for a key $k$. For all keys in-between such a pair of samples, Diva removes their longest common prefix since it can be inferred from the trie and is thus redundant.

Keys in the denser regions of the key space have longer common prefixes. Therefore, removing the longest common prefixes saves more memory in these regions. As we will see shortly, Diva exploits this and encodes the keys in such regions at a higher resolution by also storing their lower-order bits, losing less information. This enables accurately answering fine-grained queries over dense regions while supporting coarse-grained queries over sparse regions.

Given the $i$-th and $(i + 1)$-th samples as the predecessor and successor, we denote the length of their longest common prefix, in bits, by $m_i^{shared}$. Figure 3-A shows an example where the longest common prefix of the predecessor and successor is $m_i^{shared}=4$ bits long. If the samples have different lengths, Diva treats the shorter one as having trailing zeros to match the length of the longer one.

**Deriving Infixes.** To further curb memory footprint, Diva also truncates suffixes for all keys between two samples. What remains of each key is called an Infix since it is a sequence of adjacent bits in the middle of the original key. All infixes in the filter have the same length, denoted by $m^{infix}$. Due to the removed common prefix, infixes represent less significant bits of the keys in dense regions of the key space and more significant bits in sparser ones. Truncating keys into fixed-length infixes discretizes the range between two samples while removing the variation in length of the keys. As we will see, Diva supports range queries over these infixes by correspondingly discretizing the query boundaries and checking for the inclusion of infixes between them. The fixed length of the infixes allows for quickly checking for inclusion using integer arithmetic.

The length of the infixes determines the filter’s FPR. In particular, Diva guarantees an FPR of $\epsilon$ by using infixes of length $m^{infix}=\lceil\log_2\frac{2T}{\epsilon}\rceil$ bits. As we will show in Section 3.2, Diva succinctly encodes each of these infixes using only $3+\lceil\log_2\frac{1}{\epsilon}\rceil$ bits, translating to one byte for an FPR of $\approx3\%$. This FPR guarantee holds for any dataset sampled from a “well-behaved” distribution. This property is suitable for a practical range filter since datasets in practice often follow common distributions, such as the Normal distribution. We formally prove this FPR guarantee in Section 4.

Figure 3-A shows an example with keys of varying lengths and $m^{infix}=7$. As $m_i^{shared}=4$, Diva derives $k$’s infix as the 7 bits following its first 4 bits, i.e., the string $(0101001)_2$.

**Distinguishing Infixes.** There is a common scenario in practice where infixes derived with the method described above have bits that do not contribute to filtering. Intuitively, if the considered predecessor and successor are still close to each other after removing their longest common prefix, the discretized key space between them becomes smaller than desired. As a result, infixes of keys in-between can become less distinguishable, reducing filtering accuracy. This phenomenon occurs when many consecutive bits after the first differentiating bit in the predecessor and successor are all 1s and 0s, respectively. In particular, in well-behaved distributions such as Normal and Zipfian, there is at least one such bit on average due to the randomness in the samples after their longest common prefix. If not removed, these bits can increase the FPR by $2\times$ compared to the case where infixes are differentiated well. The predecessor and successor in Figure 3-B represent an example.

Diva prevents this by identifying the longest matching sequence of 1s from the predecessor and 0s from the successor after their first differentiating bit. We denote the length of this sequence for the $i$-th and $(i + 1)$-th samples by $m_i^{redundant}$. Diva removes the bits corresponding to this sequence from each key between the samples. These bits are redundant, as they do not carry useful information for comparing the keys between the $i$-th and $(i + 1)$-th samples beyond what the first differentiating bit provides. Diva derives the infix of each key as its first differentiating bit concatenated with its first $m^{infix}-1$ bits after its redundant bits. Intuitively, each of these bits doubles the size of the discretized space between two samples, allowing to better differentiate the infixes in-between. The following lemma formalizes this intuition, arguing that the removal of the redundant bits fully addresses the differentiation issue:

**Lemma 3.1.** After removing the $m_i^{redundant}$ bits, there are at least $\frac{T}{\epsilon}$ and at most $\frac{2T}{\epsilon}$ different and valid values that infixes of keys between two samples can take.

Diva is able to reconstruct the redundant bits removed from an infix. It does so by recomputing $m_i^{redundant}$ from its predecessor and successor in the trie. It adds $m_i^{redundant}$ bits between the infix’s first and second bits, each equal to the negation of its first bit. This results in the original sequence of bits in the key since the redundant bits of an infix always equal the negation of its first bit.

Figure 3-B shows an example derivation of a key $k$’s infix with $m^{infix}=7$. Here, the longest common prefix of the predecessor and successor is $m_i^{shared}=4$ bits long. After removing the $m_i^{redundant}$ bits, Diva appends the next 6 bits in $k$ (i.e., 101001) to the first differentiating bit (i.e., 0), yielding the infix $(0101001)_2=41$.

**Infix Uniformity.** The samples in the trie behave similarly to the boundaries of an equi-depth histogram. When the dataset’s distribution is well-behaved, the higher-order bits of the samples capture the overall shape of the distribution and the lower-order bits of the keys in-between behave like noise. Thus, keys falling into the same bin and are almost uniformly distributed, implying that their infixes are also uniformly distributed. We formally prove this property in Section 4. This uniformity allows infixes to be treated as hash digests. We will use this property to design an efficient data structure for storing the infixes in-between a pair of samples.

**Figure 3: Diva derives key $k$’s infix by removing the $m_i^{shared}$ common prefix bits and the $m_i^{redundant}$ bits based on its predecessor and successor, and also truncating its suffix.**
sections.translationMarkdown:
Diva 的静态变体要求输入按排序顺序到达。实践中通常满足这一要求。例如，许多数据库表和索引被组织为有序列文件 [3, 4, 13, 26, 45]、B-Tree [14, 43] 或 LSM-Tree [15, 20, 21]。若输入未排序，用户必须在构建前对其排序。

Diva 从有序 key 集合中每隔 $T$ 个 key 采样一次，并把样本插入 trie。该 trie 近似 key 的分布，因为词典序相近的相邻样本对应 key 空间中的稠密区域，而相距较远的样本对应稀疏区域。因此，trie “学习”了数据分布。$T$ 越小，近似分布越准确，但 trie 和内存占用越大。第 4 节量化 $T$ 与近似准确度之间的关系。实验发现，$T=1024$ 能以很小的内存开销（总内存的 $\approx1\%$）提供良好的综合准确度和性能。

**删除最长公共前缀。** 考虑一对连续的 trie 样本。我们将其称为二者之间 key 的 Predecessor 和 Successor。图 1 给出了 key $k$ 的 predecessor 和 successor 示例。对于这对样本之间的所有 key，Diva 删除其最长公共前缀，因为该前缀可由 trie 推断，因而是冗余的。

key 空间稠密区域内的 key 具有更长的公共前缀。因此，删除最长公共前缀能在这些区域节省更多内存。如下文所示，Diva 利用这一点，同时存储这些区域中 key 的低位 bit，以更高分辨率对其编码并减少信息损失。这使其既能准确回答稠密区域上的细粒度查询，又能支持稀疏区域上的粗粒度查询。

给定第 $i$ 个和第 $(i+1)$ 个样本作为 predecessor 和 successor，我们用 $m_i^{shared}$ 表示其最长公共前缀的 bit 长度。图 3-A 中的示例里，predecessor 与 successor 的最长公共前缀长度为 $m_i^{shared}=4$ bit。若样本长度不同，Diva 将较短样本视为具有尾随 0，使其长度与较长样本相同。

**导出 Infix。** 为进一步减少内存占用，Diva 还截断两个样本之间所有 key 的后缀。每个 key 剩余的部分称为 Infix，因为它是原始 key 中间的一段连续 bit。Filter 中所有 infix 长度相同，记为 $m^{infix}$。由于删除了公共前缀，infix 在 key 空间的稠密区域表示 key 的较低位 bit，在稀疏区域则表示较高位 bit。把 key 截断为定长 infix，可在消除 key 长度变化的同时离散化两个样本之间的范围。Diva 相应地离散化查询边界，并检查二者之间是否包含 infix，从而支持这些 infix 上的范围查询。infix 定长使其可以使用整数运算快速检查包含关系。

infix 长度决定 filter 的 FPR。具体而言，Diva 使用长度为 $m^{infix}=\lceil\log_2\frac{2T}{\epsilon}\rceil$ bit 的 infix 来保证 FPR 为 $\epsilon$。如第 3.2 节所示，Diva 仅用 $3+\lceil\log_2\frac1\epsilon\rceil$ bit 紧凑编码每个 infix；当 FPR 为 $\approx3\%$ 时，这相当于一个 byte。该保证对从“良态”分布中采样的任意数据集成立。实践中的数据集通常遵循 Normal 等常见分布，因此该性质适合实际 range filter。第 4 节将正式证明这一 FPR 保证。

图 3-A 给出了 key 长度不同且 $m^{infix}=7$ 的示例。由于 $m_i^{shared}=4$，Diva 将 $k$ 前 4 bit 后面的 7 bit，即字符串 $(0101001)_2$，作为其 infix。

**区分 Infix。** 实践中经常出现这样的场景：用上述方法导出的 infix 包含对 filtering 没有贡献的 bit。直观而言，如果删除最长公共前缀后 predecessor 和 successor 仍然彼此接近，则二者之间离散化 key 空间会小于期望大小。因此，中间 key 的 infix 会变得更难区分，降低 filtering 准确度。当 predecessor 与 successor 中位于第一个差异 bit 之后的许多连续 bit 分别全为 1 和全为 0 时，就会出现这种现象。特别是，在 Normal 和 Zipfian 等良态分布中，由于样本在最长公共前缀后的随机性，平均至少存在一个这样的 bit。若不删除，与 infix 区分良好的情况相比，这些 bit 可使 FPR 增加 $2\times$。图 3-B 的 predecessor 和 successor 就是一个示例。

Diva 在第一个差异 bit 之后，识别 predecessor 中由 1 组成的最长序列与 successor 中由 0 组成的最长序列。我们用 $m_i^{redundant}$ 表示第 $i$ 个与第 $(i+1)$ 个样本中该序列的长度。Diva 从样本之间的每个 key 中删除与该序列对应的 bit。这些 bit 是冗余的，因为在第一个差异 bit 所提供的信息之外，它们不携带用于比较两样本之间 key 的有用信息。Diva 将每个 key 的第一个差异 bit 与冗余 bit 之后的前 $m^{infix}-1$ 个 bit 拼接，得到其 infix。直观而言，这些 bit 中每增加一个，两个样本之间离散空间的大小就翻倍，从而可以更好地区分中间的 infix。以下引理形式化了该直觉，并说明删除冗余 bit 可完全解决区分问题：

**引理 3.1。** 删除 $m_i^{redundant}$ 个 bit 后，两个样本之间 key 的 infix 至少有 $\frac{T}{\epsilon}$、至多有 $\frac{2T}{\epsilon}$ 个不同且有效的可取值。

Diva 可以重建从 infix 中删除的冗余 bit。它根据 trie 中的 predecessor 和 successor 重新计算 $m_i^{redundant}$，在 infix 的第一和第二个 bit 之间添加 $m_i^{redundant}$ 个 bit，每个 bit 都是第一个 bit 的取反。由于 infix 的冗余 bit 始终等于其第一个 bit 的取反，由此可得到 key 中原始的 bit 序列。

图 3-B 给出了在 $m^{infix}=7$ 时导出 key $k$ 的 infix 的示例。这里 predecessor 与 successor 的最长公共前缀长度为 $m_i^{shared}=4$ bit。删除 $m_i^{redundant}$ 个 bit 后，Diva 将 $k$ 中接下来的 6 bit（即 101001）附加到第一个差异 bit（即 0）之后，得到 infix $(0101001)_2=41$。

**Infix 均匀性。** trie 中的样本与等深直方图的边界类似。当数据集分布良态时，样本的高位 bit 捕获分布的整体形状，而中间 key 的低位 bit 表现得像噪声。因此，落入同一个 bin 的 key 近似均匀分布，这意味着其 infix 也均匀分布。第 4 节将正式证明该性质。这种均匀性使 infix 可以被视为 hash digest。我们将利用该性质设计一种高效的数据结构，以存储一对样本之间的 infix。

**图 3：Diva 根据 key $k$ 的 predecessor 和 successor，删除 $m_i^{shared}$ 个公共前缀 bit 和 $m_i^{redundant}$ 个 bit，并截断其后缀，从而导出 $k$ 的 infix。**

sections.title: 3.2 Infix Stores
sections.originalMarkdown:
An Infix Store is a random-access array of slots that leverages the uniformity of the infixes in-between two samples to efficiently and succinctly store them. We describe a static Infix Store variant and extend it to support dynamic operations in Section 3.5 by utilizing techniques inspired by the Rank-and-Select Quotient Filter [41].

**Quotienting.** An Infix Store applies Knuth’s quotienting technique [30] to its infixes, splitting them into Quotients and Remainders. Specifically, it takes the $\lceil\log_2\frac{2}{\epsilon}\rceil$ least-significant bits of an infix $x$ as its remainder $x_r$ while taking the rest of its bits as its quotient $x_q$. Figure 4-A illustrates this split for an infix $x=(11011)_2$. Splitting the infixes in this way allows an Infix Store to succinctly encode infixes using little extra metadata.

**Storing Quotients.** An Infix Store employs a bitmap, called the occupieds bitmap, to represent every possible quotient. The $i$-th bit in this bitmap is set to 1 if an infix with a quotient equal to $i$ exists and is set to 0 otherwise. This bitmap is $T$ bits long since there are between $T/2$ and $T$ many possible quotients.¹ One can show this by applying Lemma 3.1 and removing the $\lceil\log_2\frac{2}{\epsilon}\rceil$ least-significant bits belonging to remainders from the possible infixes to derive the range of all possible quotients. Figure 4-B shows an example of this bitmap. Here, since the Infix Store contains the infix $x$ in Figure 4-A, the bit at offset 3 of the occupieds bitmap is set to 1.

¹ One can optimize the memory consumption of the filter by storing less than $T$ bits in this bitmap whenever there are less than $T$ possible quotients. Since the infixes are uniformly distributed, this saves, on average, 0.25 BPK of memory. However, we have opted for a $T$-bit bitmap to simplify the design and implementation of Infix Stores.

**Storing Remainders.** An Infix Store places infix remainders in its array of slots. As there are exactly $T-1$ keys in-between two samples, an Infix Store allocates $T-1$ slots in this array, each $\lceil\log_2\frac{2}{\epsilon}\rceil$ bits wide, to accommodate their remainders. It stores remainders of infixes sharing the same quotient in a set of contiguous slots called a Run. Runs are stored in increasing order of their quotients. Runs are 1-2 slots long in expectation since the infixes are uniformly distributed, as described at the end of Section 3.1, and have between $T/2$ and $T$ distinct quotients.

An Infix Store employs a $(T-1)$-bit bitmap with one bit per slot, called the runends bitmap, to delimit runs in an Infix Store. The $i$-th bit of this bitmap is 1 if the $i$-th slot in the Infix Store is the last slot of a run and is 0 otherwise. Figure 4-B shows an example of runs, depicted as boxes, and the runends bitmap delimiting them.

In total, the occupieds and runends bitmaps consume 2 bits per slot in the array of slots, or equivalently, 2 bits per infix.

**Matching Invariant.** Each quotient, i.e., a 1 in the occupieds bitmap, is associated with exactly one run in an Infix Store. Since runs are in increasing quotient order, a Matching Invariant holds: the run associated with the $i$-th 1 in the occupieds bitmap ends at the slot corresponding to the $i$-th 1 in the runends bitmap. Figure 4-B illustrates this invariant with bidirectional arrows.

**Locating Runs.** Diva locates an infix’s run by first checking the bit corresponding to its quotient in the occupieds bitmap. If it is 0, then the infix does not have a run. Otherwise, it leverages the one-to-one correspondences in the matching invariant to locate the end of its run, allowing for processing queries via a right-to-left scan of an average of 1-2 slots. Following a similar process, Diva is able to reconstruct all the infixes by concatenating each quotient in the occupieds bitmap with the remainders in its run.

Diva speeds up locating an infix’s run by employing rank and select primitives [41]. Formally, let $rank(i,B)$ be the number of 1s before the $i$-th bit in a bitmap $B$ and $select(i,B)$ be the position of the $i$-th 1 bit in $B$. Diva finds the last slot in an infix $x$’s run by evaluating $select(rank(x_q,occupieds),runends)$. In other words, it determines the run number it must jump to by computing rank over the occupieds bitmap and uses the result to locate the corresponding runends bits by computing select. Figure 4-B shows an example of this derivation with $x_q=3$. Diva employs specialized hardware instructions [41] to efficiently apply rank and select.

**Infix Store Range Queries.** An Infix Store processes a range query over infixes $q=[l,r]$ by finding the quotients and remainders of the endpoints and considering the following two cases:

If the range spans a single quotient ($l_q=r_q$), Diva checks if a run corresponding to that quotient exists. If not, Diva returns a negative, as no infix could be in the query range. Otherwise, it goes to the end of the run and scans it from right to left until it reaches either a new run or the start of the Infix Store. Diva determines if it has reached a new run using the runends bitmap. During this scan, Diva searches for a remainder between the remainders of the endpoints $l_r$ and $r_r$. If found, it returns a positive and a negative otherwise. This scan is very efficient, as the expected size of a run is 1-2 slots. Query $q$ in Figure 4-C is an example. Here, $l_q=r_q=6$ and the bit at position 6 of the occupieds bitmap is one. Thus, Diva searches the relevant run for a remainder between $l_r=(101)_2$ and $r_r=(110)_2$. Since $rank(6,occupieds)=3$ and $select(3,runends)=6$, Diva checks the slots from Slot 6 backward until it exhausts the run. It returns a negative, as it does not find a remainder in the desired range.

If the range spans multiple quotients ($l_q<r_q$), Diva checks if any quotient strictly between the endpoint quotients exists using the occupieds bitmap. If there is such a quotient, Diva answers with a positive, as all of the infixes in its run are strictly in the query range. Otherwise, it checks if any remainder in the left endpoint’s run is larger than its remainder $l_r$, or if any remainder in the right endpoint’s run is smaller than its remainder $r_r$. If either condition holds, Diva reports a positive and a negative otherwise. Query $q'$ in Figure 4-C shows an example. Here, Diva first checks the range of bits $[l'_q+1,r'_q-1]=[2,2]$ in the occupieds bitmap for ones. As there is no bit set to 1 in that range, Diva scans the remainders in $l'_q$ and $r'_q$’s runs and compares them to $l'_r$ and $r'_r$. As the former run has a remainder equal to $l'_r=(111)_2$, Diva returns a positive.

False positives occur when query and key infixes collide, which is most likely when queries follow the dataset’s distribution. Diva’s trie learns this distribution, enabling a low FPR in this difficult setting and potentially a better FPR in other workloads.

**Infix Store Point Queries.** Point queries are equivalent to specialized range queries having equal endpoints. They are thus handled by following the first case of the range query algorithm.

**Figure 4: Diva splits an infix into a remainder and a quotient (Part A). It encodes the quotient in the occupieds bitmap while storing the remainder in contiguous runs in the array of slots, delineated using thick lines (Part B). Diva handles a query by locating the corresponding runs using rank and select operations, followed by scanning them (Part C). The remainders that satisfy the queries are highlighted.**
sections.translationMarkdown:
Infix Store 是一个由 slot 组成的随机访问数组，它利用两个样本之间 infix 的均匀性，高效且紧凑地存储这些 infix。我们先描述 Infix Store 的静态变体，并在第 3.5 节中利用受 Rank-and-Select Quotient Filter [41] 启发的技术，将其扩展为支持动态操作的版本。

**Quotienting。** Infix Store 对 infix 应用 Knuth 的 quotienting 技术 [30]，将其拆分为 Quotient 和 Remainder。具体而言，它取 infix $x$ 的最低 $\lceil\log_2\frac{2}{\epsilon}\rceil$ 个 bit 作为 remainder $x_r$，其余 bit 则作为 quotient $x_q$。图 4-A 展示了对 infix $x=(11011)_2$ 进行这种拆分的例子。以这种方式拆分 infix，使 Infix Store 只需使用很少的额外 metadata 就能紧凑地编码 infix。

**存储 Quotient。** Infix Store 使用一个称为 occupieds bitmap 的 bitmap 来表示每一个可能的 quotient。如果存在 quotient 等于 $i$ 的 infix，则该 bitmap 的第 $i$ 个 bit 被置为 1，否则置为 0。该 bitmap 的长度为 $T$ bit，因为可能的 quotient 数量介于 $T/2$ 和 $T$ 之间。¹ 可以通过应用引理 3.1，并从可能的 infix 中删除属于 remainder 的最低 $\lceil\log_2\frac{2}{\epsilon}\rceil$ 个 bit，推导出所有可能 quotient 的取值范围，从而证明这一点。图 4-B 展示了该 bitmap 的示例。在这个例子中，由于 Infix Store 包含图 4-A 中的 infix $x$，因此 occupieds bitmap 中 offset 为 3 的 bit 被置为 1。

¹ 每当可能的 quotient 少于 $T$ 个时，可以在该 bitmap 中存储少于 $T$ 个 bit，从而优化 filter 的内存消耗。由于 infix 均匀分布，这样做平均可以节省 0.25 BPK 内存。然而，为了简化 Infix Store 的设计与实现，我们选择使用 $T$-bit bitmap。

**存储 Remainder。** Infix Store 将 infix 的 remainder 放在其 slot 数组中。由于两个样本之间恰好有 $T-1$ 个 key，因此 Infix Store 在该数组中分配 $T-1$ 个 slot，每个 slot 的宽度为 $\lceil\log_2\frac{2}{\epsilon}\rceil$ bit，以容纳这些 remainder。具有相同 quotient 的 infix 的 remainder 被存储在一组连续 slot 中，这组 slot 称为 Run。Run 按其 quotient 的递增顺序存储。如第 3.1 节末尾所述，由于 infix 均匀分布，并且具有 $T/2$ 到 $T$ 个不同的 quotient，因此 Run 的期望长度为 1–2 个 slot。

Infix Store 使用一个 $(T-1)$-bit bitmap 来划分其中的 Run；该 bitmap 中每个 slot 对应一个 bit，称为 runends bitmap。如果 Infix Store 中第 $i$ 个 slot 是某个 Run 的最后一个 slot，则该 bitmap 的第 $i$ 个 bit 为 1，否则为 0。图 4-B 给出了 Run 的示例，其中方框表示 Run，而 runends bitmap 划分了这些 Run 的边界。

总体而言，occupieds bitmap 和 runends bitmap 在 slot 数组中对每个 slot 消耗 2 bit，等价地说，对每个 infix 消耗 2 bit。

**Matching Invariant。** 每个 quotient，也就是 occupieds bitmap 中的一个 1，都恰好与 Infix Store 中的一个 Run 相关联。由于 Run 按 quotient 的递增顺序排列，因此满足一个 Matching Invariant：与 occupieds bitmap 中第 $i$ 个 1 相关联的 Run，结束于 runends bitmap 中第 $i$ 个 1 所对应的 slot。图 4-B 使用双向箭头展示了该 invariant。

**定位 Run。** Diva 首先检查 occupieds bitmap 中与某个 infix 的 quotient 对应的 bit，以定位该 infix 的 Run。如果该 bit 为 0，那么该 infix 没有对应的 Run。否则，Diva 利用 Matching Invariant 中的一一对应关系定位其 Run 的末端，从而可以通过平均扫描 1–2 个 slot 的从右向左扫描来处理查询。采用类似过程，Diva 还可以把 occupieds bitmap 中的每个 quotient 与其 Run 中的 remainder 拼接起来，从而重建所有 infix。

Diva 使用 rank 和 select primitive [41] 加快 infix Run 的定位。形式化地，令 $rank(i,B)$ 表示 bitmap $B$ 中第 $i$ 个 bit 之前的 1 的数量，令 $select(i,B)$ 表示 $B$ 中第 $i$ 个值为 1 的 bit 的位置。Diva 通过计算 $select(rank(x_q,occupieds),runends)$，找到 infix $x$ 的 Run 中最后一个 slot。换言之，它在 occupieds bitmap 上计算 rank，确定必须跳转到的 Run 编号，然后使用该结果在 runends 中计算 select，以定位相应的 runends bit。图 4-B 给出了 $x_q=3$ 时这一推导过程的示例。Diva 使用专用硬件指令 [41] 高效执行 rank 和 select。

**Infix Store 范围查询。** Infix Store 处理 infix 上的范围查询 $q=[l,r]$ 时，先求出两个端点的 quotient 和 remainder，然后考虑以下两种情况：

如果该范围只跨越一个 quotient（$l_q=r_q$），Diva 检查与该 quotient 对应的 Run 是否存在。如果不存在，Diva 返回 negative，因为查询范围内不可能有任何 infix。否则，它移动到该 Run 的末端，并从右向左扫描，直到到达一个新的 Run 或 Infix Store 的起点。Diva 使用 runends bitmap 判断是否已经到达新的 Run。在扫描期间，Diva 搜索 remainder 位于两个端点 remainder $l_r$ 和 $r_r$ 之间的项。如果找到，就返回 positive；否则返回 negative。由于 Run 的期望大小只有 1–2 个 slot，该扫描非常高效。图 4-C 中的查询 $q$ 就是一个例子。这里 $l_q=r_q=6$，且 occupieds bitmap 中位置 6 的 bit 为 1。因此，Diva 在相关 Run 中搜索位于 $l_r=(101)_2$ 和 $r_r=(110)_2$ 之间的 remainder。由于 $rank(6,occupieds)=3$ 且 $select(3,runends)=6$，Diva 从 Slot 6 开始向后检查 slot，直到扫描完该 Run。由于没有在目标范围中找到 remainder，它返回 negative。

如果该范围跨越多个 quotient（$l_q<r_q$），Diva 使用 occupieds bitmap 检查两个端点 quotient 严格之间是否存在任何 quotient。如果存在这样的 quotient，Diva 返回 positive，因为其 Run 中的所有 infix 都严格位于查询范围内。否则，它检查左端点 Run 中是否有任何 remainder 大于左端点 remainder $l_r$，或者右端点 Run 中是否有任何 remainder 小于右端点 remainder $r_r$。只要任一条件成立，Diva 就返回 positive；否则返回 negative。图 4-C 中的查询 $q'$ 给出了一个例子。这里，Diva 首先检查 occupieds bitmap 中 bit 范围 $[l'_q+1,r'_q-1]=[2,2]$ 内是否存在 1。由于该范围中没有被置为 1 的 bit，Diva 扫描 $l'_q$ 和 $r'_q$ 的 Run 中的 remainder，并分别将它们与 $l'_r$ 和 $r'_r$ 比较。由于前一个 Run 中有一个 remainder 等于 $l'_r=(111)_2$，Diva 返回 positive。

当查询 infix 与 key infix 发生碰撞时会产生假阳性；当查询服从数据集的分布时，这种碰撞最有可能发生。Diva 的 trie 学习了这一分布，使其在这种困难场景中仍能获得较低的 FPR，并可能在其他 workload 中获得更好的 FPR。

**Infix Store 点查询。** 点查询等价于两个端点相同的特殊范围查询。因此，它们按照范围查询算法的第一种情况处理。

**图 4：Diva 将一个 infix 拆分为 remainder 和 quotient（A 部分）。它在 occupieds bitmap 中编码 quotient，同时把 remainder 存储在 slot 数组的连续 Run 中，图中使用粗线标出各 Run 的边界（B 部分）。Diva 使用 rank 和 select 操作定位相应的 Run，然后扫描这些 Run，以处理查询（C 部分）。满足查询的 remainder 已被高亮。**

sections.title: 3.3 Trie Choice
sections.originalMarkdown:
Diva can use any data structure supporting predecessor and successor searches as its trie. This structure must also associate a pointer to an Infix Store with each sample. The samples and the extra pointers result in a negligible overhead of $O(L)+64$ bits per sample, where $L$ is the average key length. This translates to a total overhead of $\frac{O(L)+64}{T}\approx0.06+O(L/T)$ BPK. Using a traditional trie [9, 32, 34] for this purpose yields poor performance for predecessor and successor queries, as it incurs $O(L)$ cache misses for tree traversal.

**$y$-Fast Tries.** Instead of a traditional trie, one can employ a $y$-Fast trie [48] to achieve an $O(\log_2L)$ number of cache misses for predecessor and successor queries. A $y$-Fast trie enables this by partitioning the ordered set of keys into groups of $\approx L$ consecutive keys and storing each group in a balanced binary search tree. It efficiently accesses these groups by storing their boundary keys in a binary trie and doing predecessor/successor searches over it.

The nodes in this binary trie are represented as a hash table storing all prefixes of the boundary keys. Each inner node without a left (resp. right) child stores a pointer to the predecessor (resp. successor) of the minimum (resp. maximum) key in its subtree, allowing for fast predecessor and successor searches. This binary trie finds the predecessor/successor of a key by determining its longest prefix with a node in the trie via binary search. If the found node is a leaf node, it is returned as the answer. Otherwise, the predecessor/successor pointers are followed to answer the query.

A $y$-Fast trie processes a predecessor/successor query by doing a predecessor/successor search over its binary trie to find the corresponding binary search tree and searching in it.

Searching the binary trie and the associated binary search tree each incurs $O(\log_2L)$ cache misses, resulting in $O(\log_2L)$ cache misses for a predecessor/successor query. This cost applies out-of-the-box to Diva’s queries since searching an Infix Store incurs only a constant number of cache misses due to its small size, leaving the search cost of the trie as the main bottleneck.

A $y$-Fast trie supports amortized $O(\log_2L)$ insertions and deletions by merging and splitting the binary search trees to maintain a size of $\approx L$ for each and updating its binary trie.

**Wormhole.** As our $y$-Fast trie implementation, we use Wormhole [49], a $y$-Fast trie with a high fanout. We extended Wormhole with bidirectional iterators to quickly traverse the trie.
sections.translationMarkdown:
Diva 可以使用任何支持 predecessor 和 successor search 的数据结构作为其 trie。该结构还必须把一个指向 Infix Store 的指针与每个样本关联起来。样本和额外指针对每个样本只产生可忽略的 $O(L)+64$ bit 开销，其中 $L$ 为平均 key 长度。这对应的总开销为 $\frac{O(L)+64}{T}\approx0.06+O(L/T)$ BPK。若为此使用传统 trie [9, 32, 34]，predecessor 和 successor 查询的性能会很差，因为树遍历会产生 $O(L)$ 次 cache miss。

**$y$-Fast Trie。** 可以使用 $y$-Fast trie [48] 代替传统 trie，使 predecessor 和 successor 查询只产生 $O(\log_2L)$ 次 cache miss。$y$-Fast trie 将有序 key 集合划分为多组连续 key，每组包含约 $L$ 个 key，并将每组存储在一棵平衡 binary search tree 中，从而实现这一点。它把各组的边界 key 存储在一棵 binary trie 中，并在其上执行 predecessor/successor search，从而高效访问这些组。

该 binary trie 的节点使用一个存储所有边界 key 前缀的 hash table 表示。每个没有左孩子（相应地，没有右孩子）的内部节点，都会存储一个指向其子树中最小 key 的 predecessor（相应地，最大 key 的 successor）的指针，从而实现快速 predecessor 和 successor search。该 binary trie 通过 binary search 确定某个 key 的最长前缀，使该前缀对应 trie 中的一个节点，由此找到该 key 的 predecessor/successor。如果找到的节点是叶节点，就将其作为答案返回；否则，沿 predecessor/successor 指针得到查询答案。

$y$-Fast trie 处理 predecessor/successor 查询时，先在其 binary trie 上执行 predecessor/successor search，找到相应的 binary search tree，然后在该树中进行搜索。

搜索 binary trie 和与之关联的 binary search tree 各自产生 $O(\log_2L)$ 次 cache miss，因此一次 predecessor/successor 查询总共产生 $O(\log_2L)$ 次 cache miss。该成本可直接应用于 Diva 的查询，因为 Infix Store 大小很小，搜索它只产生常数次 cache miss，所以 trie 的搜索成本成为主要瓶颈。

$y$-Fast trie 通过合并和拆分 binary search tree，使每棵树的大小维持在约 $L$，并更新其 binary trie，从而支持摊销成本为 $O(\log_2L)$ 的插入和删除。

**Wormhole。** 我们使用高 fanout 的 $y$-Fast trie Wormhole [49] 作为 $y$-Fast trie 实现。我们为 Wormhole 扩展了双向 iterator，以快速遍历 trie。

sections.title: 3.4 Construction
sections.originalMarkdown:
The static variant of Diva is constructed using an efficient bulk-loading procedure where a sorted set of keys is scanned once. Here, Diva inserts every $T$-th key into the trie. Since it encounters the keys in-between in increasing order, it also sees their infixes in increasing order. During this scan, Diva sets the bits in the occupieds bitmap corresponding to the quotients it sees to 1 while sequentially creates the runs. Whenever it encounters a new quotient or fills the Infix Store, it marks the end of the current run in the runends bitmap by setting the bit corresponding to the last filled slot to 1.

This process incurs $O(N)$ cache misses for scanning the sorted key set and inserting infixes into Infix Stores, and $O(N\cdot\frac{\log_2L}{T})$ cache misses for inserting every $T$-th key as a sample into the trie, totaling to $O(N\cdot(1+\frac{\log_2L}{T}))$ cache misses. Since $\frac{\log_2L}{T}\ll1$, we can rewrite the cost as $O(N)$. The left-most column in Table 3 compares the construction times of the filters and the penultimate row expresses this cost for Diva.

Practically, Diva leverages the hardware prefetcher to the fullest during bulk-loading, as all its memory accesses are sequential. Its CPU overhead is also minimal, as it makes no use of hashing, save for the hashing of the trie, allowing Diva to enjoy a much faster construction time than its competitors.
sections.translationMarkdown:
Diva 的静态变体使用一种高效的 bulk-loading 过程构建，该过程只扫描已排序 key 集合一次。在此过程中，Diva 将每第 $T$ 个 key 插入 trie。由于它按递增顺序遇到中间的 key，因此也按递增顺序看到这些 key 的 infix。扫描期间，Diva 将 occupieds bitmap 中与所见 quotient 对应的 bit 置为 1，同时顺序创建 Run。每当遇到新的 quotient 或 Infix Store 被填满时，它就把 runends bitmap 中与最后一个已填充 slot 对应的 bit 置为 1，以标记当前 Run 的末端。

该过程扫描已排序 key 集合并将 infix 插入 Infix Store，产生 $O(N)$ 次 cache miss；每第 $T$ 个 key 作为样本插入 trie，产生 $O(N\cdot\frac{\log_2L}{T})$ 次 cache miss；总计产生 $O(N\cdot(1+\frac{\log_2L}{T}))$ 次 cache miss。由于 $\frac{\log_2L}{T}\ll1$，可将该成本改写为 $O(N)$。表 3 最左侧一列比较了各 filter 的构建时间，其中倒数第二行给出了 Diva 的这一成本。

在实际运行中，由于所有内存访问都是顺序的，Diva 在 bulk-loading 期间可以充分利用硬件 prefetcher。它的 CPU 开销也很低，因为除了对 trie 执行 hashing 之外，它不使用 hashing；因此，Diva 的构建速度远快于竞争方法。

sections.title: 3.5 Dynamicity
sections.originalMarkdown:
Diva generalizes to dynamic key sets, providing full support for insertions, deletions, and growing datasets.

**Dynamic Infix Stores.** Diva modifies its Infix Stores to support dynamic insertions and deletions using three techniques: 1) it overprovisions slots in each Infix Store to absorb new insertions, 2) it evenly spaces out the runs using a linear mapping function to uniformly place them in the array of slots according to their quotients, and 3) it handles insertions and deletions by shifting the runs to the right or left to create space for remainders, similarly to Robin-Hood Hashing [11].

Inserting and deleting infixes may cause Infix Stores to overflow or underflow. Diva handles such cases by resizing the array of slots in the Infix Store and updating its linear mapping function to maintain the even spacing of the runs.

**Sampling New Keys.** Diva maintains its distribution model by sampling new keys and updating its trie, “splitting” large Infix Stores in the process. Specifically, with probability $\frac1T$, Diva inserts a new key as a sample into the trie, and with probability $(1-\frac1T)$, it inserts the key’s infix into its Infix Store. This choice of probabilities ensures a high-resolution distribution model that keeps Infix Stores at an average size of $T$, keeping runs short and controlling the FPR. Randomization is essential here since we would like to store full keys in the trie to approximate the key distribution closely. Diva can only access full keys during insertions, so it must sample them on the fly and cannot split Infix Stores along their median like B-Trees.

**Splitting and Merging Infix Stores.** When sampling a new key, Diva splits the Infix Store it would have otherwise fallen into and sends the infixes of smaller and larger keys to separate Infix Stores. This keeps Infix Stores small and fast to update. Analogously, Diva merges two neighboring Infix Stores when deleting the sample between them from the trie.

**Variable-Length Infixes.** Inserting new samples into the trie brings the predecessors and successors of the keys closer together. As a result, old infixes may not carry the required fine-grained information to differentiate their keys due to longer common prefixes and an increased number of redundant bits, making them shorter. Yet, Diva stores as much information as possible about new keys by inserting them as full-length infixes to control the FPR. Diva encodes these variable-length infixes in the same Infix Store by padding them to the same length with unary padding consisting of 0s delimited by a 1, similarly to recent expandable filters [5, 18, 19, 23]. Padded infixes are one bit longer than full-length infixes because they have the delimiter bit, requiring slots that are one bit wider in the Infix Stores and incurring a memory overhead of 1 BPK. However, the shorter infixes (and fingerprints in expandable filters) cause the FPR to increase logarithmically with the data size [19, 23]. One can maintain a stable FPR at all times by widening the slots in the Infix Stores by $O(\log_2 \log_2 N)$ bits after $N$ insertions to store progressively longer infixes, similarly to InfiniFilter’s Widening Regime [18, 19, 39].

**Queries.** Point and range queries are processed similarly to the static case, with the only difference being that the missing bits from shorter infixes are treated as wildcards.
sections.translationMarkdown:
Diva 可推广到动态 key 集合，完整支持插入、删除以及持续增长的数据集。

**动态 Infix Store。** Diva 使用三种技术修改 Infix Store，以支持动态插入和删除：1）在每个 Infix Store 中超额配置 slot，以吸收新的插入；2）使用线性映射函数，根据 Run 的 quotient 将它们均匀地放置在 slot 数组中，从而使各 Run 均匀间隔；3）与 Robin-Hood Hashing [11] 类似，通过将 Run 向右或向左移动，为 remainder 腾出空间，以处理插入和删除。

插入和删除 infix 可能导致 Infix Store overflow 或 underflow。Diva 通过调整 Infix Store 中 slot 数组的大小，并更新其线性映射函数来处理这些情况，以保持 Run 之间的均匀间距。

**采样新 Key。** Diva 通过采样新 key 并更新其 trie 来维护分布模型，同时在此过程中“拆分”大型 Infix Store。具体而言，Diva 以概率 $\frac1T$ 将一个新 key 作为样本插入 trie，并以概率 $(1-\frac1T)$ 将该 key 的 infix 插入其 Infix Store。选择这组概率可以确保分布模型具有高分辨率，并使 Infix Store 的平均大小维持为 $T$，从而保持较短的 Run 并控制 FPR。随机化在这里至关重要，因为我们希望在 trie 中存储完整 key，以精确近似 key 分布。Diva 只能在插入期间访问完整 key，因此必须在线采样这些 key，而不能像 B-Tree 那样沿 Infix Store 的中位数将其拆分。

**拆分与合并 Infix Store。** 对一个新 key 进行采样时，Diva 拆分该 key 原本会落入的 Infix Store，并将较小 key 和较大 key 的 infix 分别送入不同的 Infix Store。这使 Infix Store 保持较小规模，并且可以快速更新。类似地，当从 trie 中删除两个 Infix Store 之间的样本时，Diva 会合并这两个相邻的 Infix Store。

**变长 Infix。** 向 trie 插入新样本，会使 key 的 predecessor 和 successor 彼此更加接近。因此，由于公共前缀变长且冗余 bit 数量增加，旧 infix 会变得更短，并且可能无法携带区分其 key 所需的细粒度信息。不过，Diva 将新 key 作为全长 infix 插入，尽可能多地存储有关新 key 的信息，以控制 FPR。与近期的 expandable filter [5, 18, 19, 23] 类似，Diva 在同一个 Infix Store 中编码这些变长 infix：使用由 1 作为分隔符的若干个 0 构成的一元 padding，将它们补齐到相同长度。由于 padded infix 包含 delimiter bit，因此比全长 infix 多一个 bit；这要求 Infix Store 中的 slot 也多一个 bit 的宽度，并产生 1 BPK 的内存开销。然而，较短的 infix（以及 expandable filter 中的 fingerprint）会使 FPR 以对数方式增长，其增长取决于数据规模 [19, 23]。 在插入 $N$ 个元素后，可以将 Infix Store 中的 slot 加宽 $O(\log_2 \log_2 N)$ bit，以存储逐渐变长的 infix，从而始终维持稳定的 FPR；这与 InfiniFilter 的 Widening Regime 类似 [18, 19, 39]。

**查询。** point query 和 range query 的处理方式与静态情形相似，唯一的区别是，较短 infix 中缺失的 bit 被视为通配符。

sections.title: 4 THEORETICAL ANALYSIS AND RESULTS
sections.originalMarkdown:
We prove Diva’s FPR guarantees and show that it has low operation costs. The last two rows in Table 3 summarizes this section’s results.

**Setting.** Formally, we consider the dataset’s keys to be sampled independently from a distribution with a cumulative distribution function (CDF) $F$ and probability density function (PDF) $f$. For our analysis, we treat string keys as real numbers in the range $[0, 1)$ by interpreting their binary representation as a fractional binary value. For example, we interpret the strings $(1000)_2$ and $(1100)_2$ as the real numbers $(0.1000)_2 = 0.5$ and $(0.1100)_2 = 0.75$.

**Infix Probability.** Diva returns a false positive when a query and an input key from the distribution map to the same infix in the same Infix Store. We bound the probability of this event by calculating the probability that a specific infix exists.

Intuitively, Diva’s Infix Stores partition the key space into shorter ranges. Diva discretizes each of these ranges by deriving infixes, further partitioning it into $2T/\epsilon$ equal-width sub-ranges, one for each possible infix. Figure 5 illustrates this partitioning for the $k$-th Infix Store. The probability that a particular input key maps to a specific infix is equal to the probability that it lies in the corresponding sub-range, which depends on the distribution’s CDF. We denote this probability for infix $n$ in the $k$-th Infix Store as $P_{k,n}$. We can guarantee an FPR of at most $\epsilon$ by showing that $P_{k,n} \leq \epsilon/N$ and applying a union bound to all keys in the dataset.

**Figure 5:** Infix Stores partition the key space into short ranges. Each of these ranges is partitioned into equal-width sub-ranges, each of which is condensed into an infix.

**Example: Uniform Distribution.** To give intuition why $P_{k,n}$ is small for well-behaved distributions, we first discuss the case where the input distribution is uniform, i.e., $F(x) = x$. Here, $P_{k,n}$ is exactly equal to the length of its infix’s sub-range. That is, it is equal to $\epsilon/(2T) \cdot l_k$, where $l_k$ is the length of the range spanned by the $k$-th Infix Store, as shown in Figure 5. Since there are $N/T$ many Infix Stores, $l_k$ is in expectation $T/N$. Thus, the probability of an input key landing in this sub-range is equal to $\epsilon/(2N)$, which is less than $\epsilon/N$.

**Well-Behaved Distributions.** In general, the CDF of the distribution could make $P_{k,n}$ arbitrarily large, depending on how abruptly it changes within that particular sub-range. Nevertheless, we can make a similar argument for well-behaved distributions, defined as:

**Definition 4.1 (Well-Behaved Distribution).** Let $f'$ be the derivative of $f$. A distribution is well-behaved if, for $\delta = T \cdot \log N/N$, a constant $\alpha \in [0, 1/4]$ exists such that for all $x$, $\delta^\alpha \leq f(x) \leq \delta^{-1+4\alpha}$ and $f'(x) \leq \frac{1}{3} \cdot \delta^{-\alpha}$.

The conditions above make well-behaved distributions “smooth.” The condition on $f$ keeps the distribution’s CDF from radically changing, while the condition on $f'$ limits the acceleration of change. Together, they control how much the CDF can climb in an infix’s sub-range, which is $P_{k,n}$, as shown in Figure 5. We remark that the bounds on these quantities grow polynomially in $N$, thus becoming easier to satisfy as the dataset grows. For example, $f'$ is allowed to take values as high as $\delta^{-\alpha}/3 \geq \delta^{-1/4}/3 \approx N^{1/4}/3$. Intuitively, this is because $P_{k,n}$ corresponds to a smaller sub-range with larger $N$, so our analysis can afford less smooth distributions.

Many natural distributions are well-behaved, as one can verify by plugging their PDFs into Definition 4.1. For example, the uniform distribution is trivially well-behaved since it has $f(x) = 1$ and $f'(x) = 0$. Normal distributions with a standard deviation of $\sigma \gtrsim 3/\sqrt{\pi \cdot N^{1/5}}$ and power law distributions (which generalize the Zipfian distribution) with an exponent of $\lambda \leq \log_2 N/4$ are also well-behaved. Note that $\sigma$ and $\lambda$ often satisfy these conditions.

One can use the properties of well-behaved distributions in an argument similar to that of the uniform distribution to show that:

**Theorem 4.2.** If the input distribution is well-behaved, then, with high probability over Diva’s samples, in the static (dynamic) case, a particular infix exists with (expected) probability at most $\epsilon$.

**FPR.** We now use Theorem 4.2 to bound Diva’s FPR when the dataset comes from a well-behaved distribution.

**Theorem 4.3.** Suppose that the input distribution is well-behaved. Then the static variant of Diva returns a false positive for a point query $q$ with probability at most $\epsilon$. Moreover, it returns a false positive for a range query $[q_l, q_r]$ with a probability of at most $2\epsilon$.

**Proof.** Consider the point query $q$. Since we are analyzing the probability of a false positive, we can suppose that $q$ is not in the dataset and thus does not equal any sample in Diva’s trie. Therefore, Diva checks if an infix corresponding to $q$ exists to answer the query. By Theorem 4.2, such an infix exists with a probability of at most $\epsilon$, implying an FPR of at most $\epsilon$ for $q$.

Now, consider the range query $[q_l, q_r]$. As before, we can suppose that this is an empty range, implying that it includes none of the samples in the trie and thus falls in a single Infix Store. Denoting the infixes corresponding to $q_l$ and $q_r$ by $l$ and $r$, one can see that there must be no other infix in the Infix Store that is strictly between $l$ and $r$, as it would cause the filter to return a true positive. Thus, Diva can only mistakenly return a false positive if some infix equals either $l$ or $r$. By applying Theorem 4.2, one can show that this happens with a probability of at most $2\epsilon$. □

One can analyze the dynamic variant of Diva by following a proof similar to that of Theorem 4.3. More specifically, since the filter is initially constructed using the static bulk-loading method, it starts out with point and range query FPRs of $\epsilon$ and $2\epsilon$. After inserting $N$ new keys into the filter with no distribution shifts, one can use an analysis similar to that of InfiniFilter [19] to show that:

**Theorem 4.4.** The dynamic variant of Diva returns a false positive for point query $q$ with expected probability at most $\epsilon/2 \cdot (\log_2 N + 2)$. Moreover, it returns a false positive for a range query $[q_l, q_r]$ with expected probability at most $\epsilon \cdot (\log_2 N + 2)$.

**Memory Footprint.** Both Diva variants keep a $1/T$ fraction of the keys in their trie. For each, they also store a total of 96 bits of metadata, representing whether the sample is a partial sample, the size of the Infix Store to its right, the number of infixes it stores, and a pointer to its array. Assuming that the keys have an average length of $L$ bits, the trie consumes a total of $(96+L)\cdot N/T$ bits.

In the static variant, each Infix Store has $T$ slots. It consumes $T$ bits for the occupieds bitmap, one bit per slot for the runends bitmap, and $\log_2(2/\epsilon)$ bits for each slot. Since there are a total of $N/T$ Infix Stores, the total memory footprint of the Infix Stores is $N/T \cdot (T \cdot (3+\log_2(1/\epsilon)))$ bits. Summing up the trie and Infix Store costs, noting that $T = 1024$, and dividing by $N$ results in a memory footprint of $\approx 3.09 + L/1024 + \log_2(1/\epsilon)$ BPK.

In the dynamic variant, an Infix Store with $n$ infixes uses at most $n/\alpha^2$ slots. Since there are a total of $(T-1)/T \cdot N$ infixes, this translates to a total of $(T-1)/(T\cdot\alpha^2) \cdot N \approx 1/\alpha^2 \cdot N$ slots. Moreover, each slot is one bit wider than the static case to accommodate the unary counter. As there are an expected of $N/T$ Infix Stores, this results in a total memory footprint of $\approx 1.09 + L/1024 + 1/\alpha^2 \cdot (2 + \log_2(1/\epsilon))$ BPK.

**Performance.** Each of Diva’s operations searches for the predecessor and successor of the query endpoints in the trie. As Diva uses Wormhole as its underlying trie, and since Wormhole searches for an $L$-bit key using at most $O(\log_2 L)$ cache misses, this search incurs a total of $O(\log_2 L)$ cache misses. Moreover, as the occupieds and runends bitmaps are $T = 1024$ and $T/\alpha \approx 1078$ bits long, and since a typical cache line is 512 bits long, applying rank and select operations on them incurs a constant number of cache misses. Finally, reading/modifying the slots in the Infix Store incurs a single cache miss in expectation. Thus, Diva incurs an expected of $O(\log_2 L + 1) = O(\log_2 L)$ cache misses. Notice that when keys are fixed-length, the number of cache misses becomes constant.
sections.translationMarkdown:
我们证明 Diva 的 FPR 保证，并说明它的操作成本较低。表 3 的最后两行总结了本节的结果。

**设定。** 形式化地说，我们认为数据集中的 key 是从一个具有累积分布函数（CDF）$F$ 和概率密度函数（PDF）$f$ 的分布中独立采样得到的。在分析中，我们将 string key 的二进制表示解释为二进制小数，从而把它们视为区间 $[0, 1)$ 中的实数。例如，我们将字符串 $(1000)_2$ 和 $(1100)_2$ 分别解释为实数 $(0.1000)_2 = 0.5$ 和 $(0.1100)_2 = 0.75$。

**Infix 概率。** 当一个 query 和从分布中得到的某个输入 key 映射到同一 Infix Store 中的同一 infix 时，Diva 会返回 false positive。我们通过计算某个特定 infix 存在的概率来界定这一事件的概率。

直观来看，Diva 的 Infix Store 将 key space 划分为较短的 range。Diva 通过推导 infix 对每个 range 进行离散化，进一步将其划分为 $2T/\epsilon$ 个等宽 sub-range，每一种可能的 infix 对应一个 sub-range。图 5 展示了第 $k$ 个 Infix Store 的这种划分。某个输入 key 映射到特定 infix 的概率，等于它落入对应 sub-range 的概率，而该概率取决于分布的 CDF。我们将第 $k$ 个 Infix Store 中 infix $n$ 的这一概率记为 $P_{k,n}$。只要证明 $P_{k,n} \leq \epsilon/N$，并对数据集中的所有 key 应用 union bound，就能保证 FPR 至多为 $\epsilon$。

**图 5：** Infix Store 将 key space 划分为短 range。每个 range 又被划分为等宽 sub-range，每个 sub-range 被压缩为一个 infix。

**示例：均匀分布。** 为直观说明为什么对于 well-behaved distribution，$P_{k,n}$ 很小，我们首先讨论输入分布为均匀分布的情况，即 $F(x) = x$。此时，$P_{k,n}$ 恰好等于相应 infix 的 sub-range 长度。也就是说，它等于 $\epsilon/(2T) \cdot l_k$，其中 $l_k$ 是第 $k$ 个 Infix Store 所覆盖 range 的长度，如图 5 所示。由于共有 $N/T$ 个 Infix Store，$l_k$ 的期望值为 $T/N$。因此，输入 key 落入该 sub-range 的概率为 $\epsilon/(2N)$，小于 $\epsilon/N$。

**Well-Behaved Distribution。** 一般而言，分布的 CDF 可能使 $P_{k,n}$ 任意大，具体取决于它在相应 sub-range 内变化得有多突然。不过，对于如下定义的 well-behaved distribution，我们可以给出类似的论证：

**定义 4.1（Well-Behaved Distribution）。** 令 $f'$ 为 $f$ 的导数。若对于 $\delta = T \cdot \log N/N$，存在常数 $\alpha \in [0, 1/4]$，使得对所有 $x$ 都有 $\delta^\alpha \leq f(x) \leq \delta^{-1+4\alpha}$ 且 $f'(x) \leq \frac{1}{3} \cdot \delta^{-\alpha}$，则该分布是 well-behaved 的。

上述条件使 well-behaved distribution 足够“平滑”。对 $f$ 的条件防止分布的 CDF 发生剧烈变化，而对 $f'$ 的条件则限制变化的加速度。二者共同控制 CDF 在某个 infix 的 sub-range 内能够上升多少，即控制图 5 所示的 $P_{k,n}$。值得指出的是，这些量的界会随 $N$ 以多项式速度增长，因此数据集越大，这些条件越容易满足。例如，允许 $f'$ 取到 $\delta^{-\alpha}/3 \geq \delta^{-1/4}/3 \approx N^{1/4}/3$ 这么大的值。直观上，这是因为随着 $N$ 增大，$P_{k,n}$ 对应的 sub-range 更小，因此我们的分析可以容许更不平滑的分布。

许多自然分布都是 well-behaved 的，这可以通过将其 PDF 代入定义 4.1 来验证。例如，均匀分布显然是 well-behaved 的，因为它满足 $f(x) = 1$ 和 $f'(x) = 0$。标准差满足 $\sigma \gtrsim 3/\sqrt{\pi \cdot N^{1/5}}$ 的正态分布，以及指数满足 $\lambda \leq \log_2 N/4$ 的 power-law distribution（它推广了 Zipfian distribution），也都是 well-behaved 的。注意，$\sigma$ 和 $\lambda$ 通常满足这些条件。

利用 well-behaved distribution 的性质，可以采用与均匀分布类似的论证得到：

**定理 4.2。** 如果输入分布是 well-behaved 的，那么，以关于 Diva 样本的高概率，在静态（动态）情形下，某个特定 infix 的存在概率（期望概率）至多为 $\epsilon$。

**FPR。** 下面利用定理 4.2，在数据集来自 well-behaved distribution 时界定 Diva 的 FPR。

**定理 4.3。** 假设输入分布是 well-behaved 的。那么，Diva 的静态变体对 point query $q$ 返回 false positive 的概率至多为 $\epsilon$。此外，它对 range query $[q_l, q_r]$ 返回 false positive 的概率至多为 $2\epsilon$。

**证明。** 考虑 point query $q$。由于我们分析的是 false positive 的概率，可以假设 $q$ 不在数据集中，因此不等于 Diva trie 中的任何样本。于是，Diva 通过检查与 $q$ 对应的 infix 是否存在来回答该 query。根据定理 4.2，这样的 infix 存在的概率至多为 $\epsilon$，因此 $q$ 的 FPR 至多为 $\epsilon$。

现在考虑 range query $[q_l, q_r]$。与前面一样，可以假设这是一个 empty range，这意味着它不包含 trie 中的任何样本，因此落在单个 Infix Store 中。将与 $q_l$ 和 $q_r$ 对应的 infix 分别记为 $l$ 和 $r$，可以看出，Infix Store 中不可能存在严格位于 $l$ 与 $r$ 之间的其他 infix，因为那会使 filter 返回 true positive。因此，只有当某个 infix 等于 $l$ 或 $r$ 时，Diva 才可能错误地返回 false positive。应用定理 4.2 可以证明，该事件的概率至多为 $2\epsilon$。□

可以沿用与定理 4.3 类似的证明来分析 Diva 的动态变体。更具体地说，由于 filter 最初采用静态 bulk-loading 方法构造，所以 point query 和 range query 的初始 FPR 分别为 $\epsilon$ 和 $2\epsilon$。在分布不发生漂移的情况下向 filter 插入 $N$ 个新 key 后，可以采用与 InfiniFilter [19] 类似的分析证明：

**定理 4.4。** Diva 的动态变体对 point query $q$ 返回 false positive 的期望概率至多为 $\epsilon/2 \cdot (\log_2 N + 2)$。此外，它对 range query $[q_l, q_r]$ 返回 false positive 的期望概率至多为 $\epsilon \cdot (\log_2 N + 2)$。

**内存占用。** Diva 的两个变体都在 trie 中保留 $1/T$ 的 key。对于每个样本，它们还总共存储 96 bit 的 metadata，用来表示该样本是否为 partial sample、其右侧 Infix Store 的大小、其中存储的 infix 数量以及一个指向其数组的指针。假设 key 的平均长度为 $L$ bit，则 trie 总共消耗 $(96+L)\cdot N/T$ bit。

在静态变体中，每个 Infix Store 有 $T$ 个 slot。occupieds bitmap 消耗 $T$ bit；runends bitmap 每个 slot 消耗 1 bit；每个 slot 还消耗 $\log_2(2/\epsilon)$ bit。由于总共有 $N/T$ 个 Infix Store，Infix Store 的总内存占用为 $N/T \cdot (T \cdot (3+\log_2(1/\epsilon)))$ bit。将 trie 和 Infix Store 的成本相加，注意到 $T = 1024$，再除以 $N$，可得内存占用为 $\approx 3.09 + L/1024 + \log_2(1/\epsilon)$ BPK。

在动态变体中，一个包含 $n$ 个 infix 的 Infix Store 最多使用 $n/\alpha^2$ 个 slot。由于 infix 总数为 $(T-1)/T \cdot N$，这对应总共 $(T-1)/(T\cdot\alpha^2) \cdot N \approx 1/\alpha^2 \cdot N$ 个 slot。此外，为容纳 unary counter，每个 slot 比静态情形多 1 bit。由于 Infix Store 的期望数量为 $N/T$，最终总内存占用为 $\approx 1.09 + L/1024 + 1/\alpha^2 \cdot (2 + \log_2(1/\epsilon))$ BPK。
