**性能。** Diva 的每项操作都会在 trie 中查找 query endpoint 的 predecessor 和 successor。Diva 使用 Wormhole 作为底层 trie，而 Wormhole 查找一个 $L$-bit key 最多产生 $O(\log_2 L)$ 次 cache miss，因此该查找总共产生 $O(\log_2 L)$ 次 cache miss。此外，occupieds 和 runends bitmap 的长度分别为 $T = 1024$ bit 和 $T/\alpha \approx 1078$ bit，而典型 cache line 为 512 bit，所以在其上执行 rank 和 select 操作只会产生常数次 cache miss。最后，读取或修改 Infix Store 中的 slot，期望只产生一次 cache miss。因此，Diva 期望产生 $O(\log_2 L + 1) = O(\log_2 L)$ 次 cache miss。注意，当 key 为定长时，cache miss 的次数变为常数。

sections.title: 5 EVALUATION
sections.originalMarkdown:
We evaluate Diva against existing range filters in static and dynamic settings in Sections 5.1 and 5.2, respectively. We also conduct end-to-end evaluations on top of WiredTiger [36], a popular B-Tree-based key-value store, in Section 5.2. We implement and open source an extensible benchmarking suite to conduct our experiments.

**Platform.** We run experiments on a Fedora 39 machine with an Intel Xeon w7-2495X processor (4.8 GHz) with 24 cores and 48 hyper-threads. Our machine has a 1920 KB L1, a 48 MB L2, and a 45 MB L3 cache, along with 64 GBs of main memory. It also has two SK Hynix 512 GB PC611 M.2 2280 80mm SSDs, which are used in the end-to-end experiments only.
sections.translationMarkdown:
我们分别在第 5.1 节和第 5.2 节的静态与动态设置下，将 Diva 与现有 range filter 进行评估比较。第 5.2 节还基于流行的 B-Tree key-value store WiredTiger [36] 进行了 end-to-end 评估。我们实现并开源了一套可扩展的 benchmark suite 来开展实验。

**平台。** 实验运行在一台 Fedora 39 机器上，该机器配备 Intel Xeon w7-2495X 处理器（4.8 GHz），具有 24 个 core 和 48 个 hyper-thread。机器拥有 1920 KB L1 cache、48 MB L2 cache、45 MB L3 cache，以及 64 GB 主存。它还配有两块 SK Hynix 512 GB PC611 M.2 2280 80mm SSD，仅用于 end-to-end 实验。

sections.title: 5.1 Static Evaluation
sections.originalMarkdown:
**Baselines.** We compare Diva to all existing range filters except for bloomRF [37], as its implementation is closed-source. As most other range filters only support integer keys, we specialize a version of Diva for integers to enable a fair comparison while still benchmarking the general-purpose version of Diva that supports variable-length keys. We implement both these versions in C++. All other baselines we compare to are implemented in C/C++ as well. We compile all filters using gcc-13.

**Integer Datasets.** Following prior work [12, 17, 23, 24, 29, 33, 37, 46, 47, 51], we employ the following synthetic and real-world [28, 35] datasets for our evaluation over integer data:

- **Uniform:** 200M uniformly sampled 64-bit integers.
- **Normal:** 200M 64-bit integer samples from $\mathcal{N}(2^{63}, 2^{50})$.
- **Books:** 200M popularity scores for books on Amazon. This dataset is heavily skewed.
- **OSM:** 200M geocoordinates from the Open Street Map. It features several densely populated regions in the key space.

**Query Workloads.** For integer datasets, we sample a start key $x$ and a length $R$ to generate the range $[x, x + R - 1]$. We vary $R$ to showcase the effect of range lengths on filter performance and FPR. For synthetic workloads, we choose the starting key $x$ by sampling from the same distribution as the dataset. For real workloads, we sample $x$ from the dataset and subsequently remove it from the set. We generate point queries by using $R = 1$.

Our workloads issue empty range queries, and we measure the FPR of each filter by dividing the number of positive results by the size of the query batch. We also conduct a separate experiment gauging performance for non-empty queries. Our benchmarks focus on filter CPU times. All workloads issue a total of 10M queries.

**Experiment 1: Integer FPR vs. Query Size Tradeoff.** The first and second rows of Figure 6 depict the FPR of our baselines with a memory budget of 10 and 16 BPK, respectively. Here, the $x$-axes vary the query sizes. The bottom row of Figure 6 compares the filters’ query latencies for the experiments of the second row. We omit query latency measurements for the first row since they are similar. Figure 6 has both solid and dotted plots for Proteus. In the solid plots, Proteus is tuned using a sample of the queries, while in the dotted plots, it is tuned with range queries with uniformly distributed endpoints, simulating a workload shift. Such workload shifts do not impact the other range filters. We tune Rosetta and Memento filter to assume a maximum range query length of $R = 128$ to keep their FPR for short range queries low and their memory footprint in line with the other filters. If one keeps the memory footprint constant and tunes Rosetta for longer ranges, its query speed deteriorates. Tuning Memento filter for longer ranges yields faster queries at the expense of the FPR.

The FPRs of robust range filters, i.e., Rosetta, Grafite, and Memento filter, approach one as the range query length increases. As mentioned in Section 2, this is due to the filters issuing extra probes in their internal structures. This also increases the number of cache misses in Rosetta and Memento filter, slowing down range queries by orders of magnitude. Due to similar reasons, REncoder’s FPR and speed also rapidly deteriorate with longer queries.

While SuRF approximately matches Diva’s query performance, its FPR is at least an order of magnitude higher across experiments, as it maximally truncates the keys in its trie. Furthermore, SuRF is unable to curb its memory footprint under some datasets. For instance, in Figure 6, SuRF is missing from the Books column since it requires at least 21 BPK to create a trie that differentiates all of the dataset’s keys, exceeding the allotted memory budget.

SNARF and Oasis+ match Diva’s FPR but exhibit $\approx 2\times$ slower queries due to their slower binary searches. They also do not support variable-length keys and dynamic operations. Moreover, in some scenarios, such as in the OSM dataset, Diva achieves better FPRs than SNARF by a factor of $\approx 2\times$. This is due to floating point errors in SNARF’s distribution model. We exclude Oasis+ from the OSM column since its construction takes more than 12 hours.

Proteus’ FPR is slightly lower than Diva’s under the Books and OSM datasets (due to some redundancy of the keys carrying over into Diva’s infixes) but is worse by orders of magnitude under the Uniform and Normal datasets, as the latter workloads. Moreover, Proteus’ dotted plots show that in the face of a workload shift, its FPR shoots up for medium to long queries, resulting in orders of magnitude higher FPRs than Diva. Although Proteus achieves faster queries than Diva in the absence of workload shifts, it becomes slower than Diva by an order of magnitude in the face of a workload shift as it performs more Bloom filter probes.

As shown in Figure 6, any filter that outperforms Diva in terms of FPR has significantly slower queries and vice-versa. Hence, Diva provides the best balance between FPR and query speed across the board for variable-length range queries. Moreover, as shown by the range size $2^0$, Diva is also competitive for point queries. As we show in subsequent experiments, Diva is also more general-purpose than its competitors since it achieves all other range filtering goals.

**Figure 6:** Diva provides the best balance between FPR and query latency for any workload across range query sizes.

**Experiment 2: Integer FPR vs. Memory Tradeoff.** Figure 7 depicts the FPRs of the baselines as we vary the memory budget while fixing the query length to $R = 2^{10}$. Due to its semi-robust FPR guarantee, Diva achieves a low FPR across the board, closely matching the best competitors. Although Proteus, SNARF, and Oasis+ exhibit better filtering under certain datasets, they have higher FPRs under others. Moreover, unlike Diva, none support variable-length keys and dynamicity. Because Diva’s memory footprint does not depend on $R$ and its metadata overhead is small, it is operational under budgets as low as 8 BPK (unlike SuRF or the robust filters).

**Figure 7:** Diva achieves the best FPR scaling with memory while being competitive under stringent memory budgets.

**Experiment 3: String FPR vs. Memory Tradeoff.** We now experiment with datasets and workloads containing variable-length keys. We use the following datasets, each consisting of 200M keys:

- **Normal:** the length of the key, in bytes, is chosen at random from the set {8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256}, and the first 64 bits of the keys follow the normal distribution $\mathcal{N}(2^{63}, 2^{50})$ while the other bits are chosen uniformly at random,
- **EnWiki:** English words extracted from Wikipedia titles [1].

Using the EnWiki dataset without modification results in a high FPR for all filters. This is an artifact of the erratic distribution of the English language, which features many “short and step-wise skews.” To address this problem, we compress the keys using Hu-Tucker coding [50], an order-preserving variant of Huffman coding, to “flatten” the distribution and make it more well-behaved before constructing the Diva and SuRF. We generate queries by sampling and removing pairs of adjacent keys from the dataset.

Figure 8 evaluates the FPR of Diva and SuRF, the only filters that support string keys, under various memory budgets. SuRF creates a shallow truncated trie to differentiate the keys in these workloads.

This, in conjunction with early termination of empty queries in the upper levels of the trie, allows SuRF to achieve marginally faster queries than Diva by 35%. However, its maximal truncation of suffixes causes SuRF’s FPR to be worse than Diva by as much as two orders of magnitude under the Normal dataset. While SuRF has a lower FPR for large memory budgets under the EnWiki dataset due to it ensuring that keys are differentiated, it is worse by $1.5\times$ for stringent budgets, which is the setting in which filters are typically most useful. In contrast, Diva truncates suffixes to different degrees for different regions of the key space, storing finer-grained information for denser regions that improve the FPR.

**Figure 8:** Diva guarantees competitive FPRs and comparable query times to SuRF when operating on strings.

**Experiment 4: Non-Empty Query Performance.** Until now, we only issued empty queries in our evaluations. We now benchmark non-empty query latency in Figure 9. The left subfigure varies the memory budget while fixing the range query length to $R = 2^{10}$, and the right subfigure varies the query size while fixing the memory budget to 16 BPK. We only consider the Uniform integer dataset, as other datasets yield similar results. Henceforth, Rosetta’s hierarchy depth and Memento filter’s suffix size are tuned to the longest range query length to enable their best performance.

Diva is faster than all range filters, except for Grafite and Memento filter, across all memory budgets and range query lengths. This is because the other filters incur many cache misses whilst binary searching or checking for existing keys in Bloom filters and tries. The closest other range filter to Diva’s speed, SuRF, is slower by as much as 35%, as it has to traverse all the levels in its trie down to a leaf node for a non-empty query. Grafite and Memento filter achieve their speed by localizing similar keys to the same memory region, forgoing support of variable-length keys and queries, both of which are supported by Diva. Rosetta, REncoder, and Proteus incur more cache misses as the memory budget or query length grows since they check more bits in their Bloom filters. In contrast, Diva’s query complexity is independent of the memory budget and query size, yielding consistently fast queries.

We have also experimented with mixed workloads, but exclude the plots, as all filters behave similarly to Figure 9, except for Proteus, which behaves similarly to the last row of Figure 6.

**Figure 9:** Diva brings competitive performance to the table when processing non-empty range queries.

**Experiment 5: Construction Times.** Figure 10 presents the construction times of the range filters on integer datasets of varying sizes under a memory budget of 16 BPK. We use the Uniform dataset, though the dataset choice has little influence on filter construction times (except for Oasis+). Diva is significantly faster to construct than all other range filters, with the closest range filter being $2.7\times$ slower. This gap is due to Diva’s sequential memory access pattern in the input array and its Infix Stores (which leverages the hardware prefetcher) and due to it only hashing the sample keys in its $y$-Fast trie, which comprise $\approx 0.1\%$ of all the keys.

**Figure 10:** Diva has the fastest construction time among range filters with a large margin.
sections.translationMarkdown:
**Baselines。** 我们将 Diva 与除 bloomRF [37] 之外的所有现有 range filter 进行比较，因为 bloomRF 的实现是闭源的。由于大多数其他 range filter 只支持 integer key，我们为 Diva 专门实现了 integer 版本，以便公平比较；同时仍对支持 variable-length key 的通用 Diva 版本进行 benchmark。我们用 C++ 实现了这两个版本。比较中的其他 baseline 也都由 C/C++ 实现。所有 filter 均使用 gcc-13 编译。

**Integer 数据集。** 沿用先前工作 [12, 17, 23, 24, 29, 33, 37, 46, 47, 51]，我们使用以下 synthetic dataset 和 real-world dataset [28, 35] 评估 integer data：

- **Uniform：** 均匀采样的 2 亿个 64-bit integer。
- **Normal：** 从 $\mathcal{N}(2^{63}, 2^{50})$ 中采样的 2 亿个 64-bit integer。
- **Books：** Amazon 上 2 亿本书的 popularity score。该数据集高度倾斜。
- **OSM：** 来自 Open Street Map 的 2 亿个地理坐标。其 key space 中存在多个高密度区域。

**Query Workload。** 对于 integer dataset，我们采样起始 key $x$ 和长度 $R$，以生成 range $[x, x + R - 1]$。我们改变 $R$，以展示 range 长度对 filter 性能和 FPR 的影响。对于 synthetic workload，起始 key $x$ 从与数据集相同的分布中采样。对于 real workload，我们从数据集中采样 $x$，随后将其从集合中删除。令 $R = 1$ 以生成 point query。

我们的 workload 发出 empty range query，并用 positive result 的数量除以 query batch 大小来测量各 filter 的 FPR。我们还进行了单独的实验来评估 non-empty query 的性能。benchmark 关注 filter 的 CPU time。每个 workload 总共发出 1000 万个 query。

**实验 1：Integer FPR 与 Query Size 的权衡。** 图 6 的第一行和第二行分别展示 baseline 在 10 BPK 和 16 BPK 内存预算下的 FPR，其中 $x$ 轴表示不同的 query size。图 6 最后一行比较第二行实验中各 filter 的 query latency。第一行的 query latency 与其相似，因此省略。图 6 中 Proteus 同时具有实线和虚线曲线。在实线曲线中，Proteus 使用 query 样本进行调优；在虚线曲线中，则使用 endpoint 均匀分布的 range query 进行调优，用于模拟 workload shift。这种 workload shift 不影响其他 range filter。我们将 Rosetta 和 Memento filter 调优为假设最大 range query 长度为 $R = 128$，以保持其短 range query 的 FPR 较低，并使其内存占用与其他 filter 一致。若保持内存占用不变并将 Rosetta 调优到更长的 range，其 query speed 会下降。将 Memento filter 调优到更长的 range，则会以增大 FPR 为代价获得更快的 query。

robust range filter（即 Rosetta、Grafite 和 Memento filter）的 FPR 随 range query 长度增大而趋近于 1。如第 2 节所述，这是因为这些 filter 会在其内部结构中进行额外 probe。这也会增加 Rosetta 和 Memento filter 的 cache miss 数量，使 range query 慢几个数量级。出于类似原因，REncoder 的 FPR 和速度也会随着 query 变长而快速恶化。

虽然 SuRF 的 query 性能与 Diva 大致相当，但由于它最大限度地截断 trie 中的 key，其 FPR 在各项实验中至少高一个数量级。此外，在某些数据集上，SuRF 无法控制内存占用。例如，在图 6 中，Books 一列没有 SuRF，因为它至少需要 21 BPK 才能创建一个能区分该数据集中所有 key 的 trie，超过了给定的内存预算。

SNARF 和 Oasis+ 的 FPR 与 Diva 相当，但由于 binary search 更慢，其 query 慢约 $2\times$。它们也不支持 variable-length key 和动态操作。此外，在 OSM 数据集等场景中，Diva 的 FPR 比 SNARF 好约 $2\times$。这是由 SNARF distribution model 中的 floating-point error 导致的。Oasis+ 的构造时间超过 12 小时，因此我们将其从 OSM 一列中排除。

在 Books 和 OSM 数据集上，Proteus 的 FPR 略低于 Diva（因为 key 的一部分冗余延续到了 Diva 的 infix 中）；但在 Uniform 和 Normal 数据集，即后两种 workload 上，Proteus 的 FPR 会差几个数量级。此外，Proteus 的虚线表明，面对 workload shift 时，其中长 query 的 FPR 会急剧上升，比 Diva 高几个数量级。没有 workload shift 时，Proteus 的 query 比 Diva 快；但面对 workload shift 时，它要执行更多 Bloom filter probe，因此反而比 Diva 慢一个数量级。

如图 6 所示，任何 FPR 优于 Diva 的 filter，其 query 都明显更慢，反之亦然。因此，对于 variable-length range query，Diva 在各种 workload 和 query size 下都实现了 FPR 与 query speed 的最佳平衡。此外，从 range size $2^0$ 的结果可以看出，Diva 在 point query 上同样具有竞争力。后续实验还将表明，Diva 达成了其他所有 range filtering 目标，因此比竞争对手更加通用。

**图 6：** 在各种 range query size 和 workload 下，Diva 都实现了 FPR 与 query latency 的最佳平衡。

**实验 2：Integer FPR 与内存的权衡。** 图 7 在将 query 长度固定为 $R = 2^{10}$ 时，通过改变内存预算展示各 baseline 的 FPR。凭借 semi-robust FPR 保证，Diva 在所有情况下都获得了较低 FPR，并且与最佳竞争对手非常接近。Proteus、SNARF 和 Oasis+ 在某些数据集上过滤效果更好，但在另一些数据集上的 FPR 更高。此外，与 Diva 不同，它们都不支持 variable-length key 和动态性。由于 Diva 的内存占用不依赖 $R$，且 metadata overhead 很小，因此在低至 8 BPK 的预算下仍可运行，而 SuRF 和 robust filter 无法做到这一点。

**图 7：** Diva 的 FPR 随内存扩展的效果最佳，同时在严格内存预算下仍具竞争力。

**实验 3：String FPR 与内存的权衡。** 接下来使用包含 variable-length key 的数据集和 workload 进行实验。我们采用以下两个数据集，每个都包含 2 亿个 key：

- **Normal：** key 的 byte 长度从集合 {8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256} 中随机选择；key 的前 64 bit 服从正态分布 $\mathcal{N}(2^{63}, 2^{50})$，其他 bit 均匀随机选择；
- **EnWiki：** 从 Wikipedia 标题中提取的英文单词 [1]。

直接使用未经修改的 EnWiki 数据集会使所有 filter 都产生较高 FPR。这是由英语语言的不规则分布造成的，其中包含许多“短且阶梯式的倾斜”。为解决这一问题，我们使用 Hu-Tucker coding [50] 压缩 key。它是 Huffman coding 的保序变体，可以在构造 Diva 和 SuRF 前将分布“拉平”，使其更加 well-behaved。我们通过采样并删除数据集中相邻的 key pair 来生成 query。

图 8 在不同内存预算下评估 Diva 和 SuRF 的 FPR；二者是仅有的支持 string key 的 filter。SuRF 创建一个浅层 truncated trie，以区分这些 workload 中的 key。

再结合在 trie 上层对 empty query 的 early termination，SuRF 的 query 比 Diva 快 35%。然而，在 Normal 数据集上，SuRF 对 suffix 的最大化截断会使其 FPR 比 Diva 最多差两个数量级。在 EnWiki 数据集的大内存预算下，SuRF 通过确保 key 可被区分而获得更低 FPR；但在严格预算下，它的 FPR 比 Diva 差 $1.5\times$，而严格预算恰恰是 filter 通常最有用的场景。相比之下，Diva 会对 key space 的不同区域以不同程度截断 suffix，为密度更高的区域存储粒度更细的信息，从而改善 FPR。

**图 8：** 在处理 string 时，Diva 保证具有竞争力的 FPR，同时 query time 与 SuRF 相当。

**实验 4：Non-Empty Query 性能。** 到目前为止，评估中只发出了 empty query。现在我们在图 9 中 benchmark non-empty query latency。左侧子图在将 range query 长度固定为 $R = 2^{10}$ 时改变内存预算，右侧子图在将内存预算固定为 16 BPK 时改变 query size。这里只考虑 Uniform integer dataset，因为其他数据集得到相似结果。从本实验开始，Rosetta 的 hierarchy depth 和 Memento filter 的 suffix size 均针对最长 range query 进行调优，以获得最佳性能。

在所有内存预算和 range query 长度下，Diva 都快于除 Grafite 和 Memento filter 之外的所有 range filter。这是因为其他 filter 在 binary search，或检查 Bloom filter 与 trie 中是否存在 key 时，会产生大量 cache miss。速度最接近 Diva 的其他 range filter 是 SuRF，但它处理 non-empty query 时必须遍历 trie 的所有层直至 leaf node，因此最慢可比 Diva 慢 35%。Grafite 和 Memento filter 通过将相似 key 定位到同一内存区域而获得速度，但代价是不支持 variable-length key 和 query，而 Diva 同时支持二者。Rosetta、REncoder 和 Proteus 会随着内存预算或 query 长度增大而检查 Bloom filter 中的更多 bit，因而产生更多 cache miss。相比之下，Diva 的 query complexity 与内存预算和 query size 无关，因此 query 始终很快。

我们也对 mixed workload 进行了实验，但没有展示曲线，因为除 Proteus 外，所有 filter 的表现都与图 9 类似；Proteus 的表现则与图 6 最后一行类似。

**图 9：** 处理 non-empty range query 时，Diva 展现出有竞争力的性能。

**实验 5：构造时间。** 图 10 展示了在 16 BPK 内存预算下，各 range filter 在不同规模 integer dataset 上的构造时间。我们使用 Uniform 数据集，不过数据集的选择对 filter 构造时间几乎没有影响（Oasis+ 除外）。Diva 的构造速度显著快于其他所有 range filter，最接近的 filter 也慢 $2.7\times$。产生这一差距，一方面是因为 Diva 对输入数组及其 Infix Store 采用顺序内存访问模式，从而利用 hardware prefetcher；另一方面是因为它只 hash $y$-Fast trie 中的 sample key，而这些 key 仅占全部 key 的 $\approx 0.1\%$。

**图 10：** Diva 的构造时间大幅领先所有其他 range filter。

sections.title: 5.2 Dynamic Evaluation
sections.originalMarkdown:
**Baselines.** We compare Diva to Rosetta [33], REncoder [24, 47], SNARF [46], and Memento filter [23], as they are the only filters that provide insertion APIs. Here, we implement an improved version of Memento filter that employs Aleph filter’s techniques [18, 40] to support infinite expansions. In the presence of deletions, we compare Diva to SNARF and Memento filter since they are the only other filters that have deletion APIs. Finally, we integrate Diva and Memento filter into WiredTiger [36] to conduct an end-to-end evaluation on a popular B-Tree-based key-value store. By default, the memory budget of each filter is set to 16 BPK.

**Datasets and Workloads.** We use the Books dataset with 200M keys. We construct the filters on a randomly chosen $1/64$ fraction of this dataset and insert the remaining keys in a random order. We issue queries of length $R = 2^{10}$ (and $R = 2^{20}$ in Experiment 6) with endpoints from the same distribution and collect measurements as the dataset grows. Other datasets result in similar measurements.

**Experiment 6: FPR, Space, and Insert Latency.** Figure 11 evaluates the baselines’ FPR, space, and insert latency as new keys are inserted. The $x$-axis shows the fraction of the dataset ingested. The top row plots the filters’ FPR, with its left and right subfigures issuing shorter and longer range queries. The left and right subfigures of the bottom row present the baselines’ memory footprint and insertion latency. We measure the memory footprint of the baselines, as some deviate from the user-defined memory budget.

Since Rosetta and REncoder are based on Bloom filters, they are not expandable. As such, new insertions increase the proportion of bits set to 1s in their Bloom filters, causing their FPR to quickly converge to one, making them useless as filters.

SNARF’s initial FPR is better than Diva by $\approx 2.1\times$, as it does not pay the extra cost of storing unary counters. However, its FPR deteriorates with insertions, becoming worse than Diva by more than $2\times$. This is because SNARF does not update its distribution model. This is also what leads to the slight drop in its memory footprint. In contrast, Diva maintains an accurate distribution model by adding samples to its trie. Moreover, SNARF’s insertion speed deteriorates with the dataset’s size due to its increasing block sizes, making it slower than Diva by as much as three orders of magnitude.

Since Memento filter does not support variable-length queries, its FPR is higher than Diva by $\approx 25.3\times$ in the top-left subfigure of Figure 11. Memento filter is missing from the top-right subfigure since it requires more than 16 BPK of memory to support these longer queries. Although Memento filter initially has faster inserts than Diva, it becomes similar to Diva as the the dataset grows due to shifting more slots in its hash table. Moreover, its memory consumption exceeds the user-defined budget at times. This is because it expands to twice its original size to accommodate new insertions, wasting as much as 50% of its capacity until it fills up again. In contrast, Diva expands its Infix Stores in small increments, maintaining a constant memory footprint and avoiding wastage.

In sum, Diva maintains the lowest FPR for range queries of any length across expansions while supporting fast insertions. It is also the only dynamic filter that respects its memory budget.

**Figure 11:** Diva has the best FPR scaling with insertions and is the only efficient filter with a stable memory footprint.

**Experiment 7: Deletions.** Figure 12-A measures the deletion performance of the filters that provide a deletion API as the dataset grows. It shows the latency of issuing 500K deletions each time the dataset size doubles. Figure 12-A demonstrates that Diva exhibits the fastest deletions, beating Memento filter and SNARF by as much as $2.54\times$ and $300\times$. SNARF’s deletion speed deteriorates with insertions since its blocks grow, forcing it to rewrite larger blocks. Memento filter also slows down since the number of slots it has to shift increases. In contrast, Diva controls the amount of shifting and rewriting it does by splitting Infix Stores.

**Experiment 8: End-to-End Query Latency.** We integrate Diva and Memento filter with WiredTiger and measure the end-to-end empty range query latency. We exclude SNARF from this experiment since it is unsuitable for dynamic workloads due to its inefficient update APIs. Figure 12-B measures end-to-end query latency each time the dataset size doubles. Each key in this workload is associated with a 504-byte value, resulting in 512-byte key-value pairs on disk. We configure WiredTiger with a buffer pool that is 1% of the data size on disk. We trade some of this memory for the range filter to draw a fair comparison. The curve labeled “Baseline” in Figure 12-B represents WiredTiger without a filter.

Diva speeds up WiredTiger’s query processing by as much as three orders of magnitude. Moreover, since it supports variable-length queries, it beats Memento filter’s query latency by $\approx 85\times$.

**Figure 12:** Diva achieves the best deletion and end-to-end query performance under dynamic workloads.
sections.translationMarkdown:
**Baselines。** 我们将 Diva 与 Rosetta [33]、REncoder [24, 47]、SNARF [46] 和 Memento filter [23] 进行比较，因为它们是仅有的提供 insertion API 的 filter。这里，我们实现了 Memento filter 的改进版本，它采用 Aleph filter 的技术 [18, 40] 来支持无限次扩展。在存在 deletion 时，我们将 Diva 与 SNARF 和 Memento filter 比较，因为只有这两种其他 filter 提供 deletion API。最后，我们将 Diva 和 Memento filter 集成进 WiredTiger [36]，在一种流行的 B-Tree key-value store 上进行 end-to-end 评估。默认情况下，每种 filter 的内存预算设为 16 BPK。

**数据集和 Workload。** 我们使用包含 2 亿个 key 的 Books 数据集。我们在从该数据集中随机选择的 $1/64$ 数据上构造 filter，然后以随机顺序插入剩余 key。我们发出 endpoint 来自相同分布、长度为 $R = 2^{10}$ 的 query（实验 6 中还使用 $R = 2^{20}$），并随着数据集增长收集测量结果。其他数据集得到的测量结果类似。

**实验 6：FPR、空间和 Insert Latency。** 图 11 评估随着新 key 插入，各 baseline 的 FPR、空间和 insert latency。$x$ 轴表示已摄取的数据集比例。第一行绘制各 filter 的 FPR，其中左、右子图分别发出较短和较长的 range query。第二行的左、右子图分别展示 baseline 的内存占用和 insertion latency。我们测量 baseline 的内存占用，是因为其中一些会偏离用户设定的内存预算。

Rosetta 和 REncoder 基于 Bloom filter，因此不可扩展。由此，新插入会增大其 Bloom filter 中被置为 1 的 bit 比例，使 FPR 很快收敛到 1，导致它们无法继续作为 filter 使用。

SNARF 的初始 FPR 比 Diva 好 $\approx 2.1\times$，因为它无需承担存储 unary counter 的额外成本。然而，其 FPR 会随着插入而恶化，最终比 Diva 差超过 $2\times$。这是因为 SNARF 不更新自己的 distribution model，也正是这个原因导致其内存占用略有下降。相比之下，Diva 通过向 trie 中添加 sample 来维持准确的 distribution model。此外，SNARF 的 block size 会随数据集增大而增大，使 insertion speed 下降，最慢时比 Diva 慢三个数量级。

由于 Memento filter 不支持 variable-length query，在图 11 左上子图中，其 FPR 比 Diva 高 $\approx 25.3\times$。右上子图中没有 Memento filter，因为支持这些更长 query 需要超过 16 BPK 的内存。虽然 Memento filter 的初始 insert 比 Diva 快，但随着数据集增长，它需要在 hash table 中移动更多 slot，因此逐渐与 Diva 相近。此外，其内存消耗有时会超过用户定义的预算。这是因为它为了容纳新插入会扩展到原大小的两倍，在再次填满前最多浪费 50% 的容量。相比之下，Diva 以小增量扩展 Infix Store，维持恒定的内存占用并避免浪费。

总而言之，在持续扩展过程中，对于任意长度的 range query，Diva 都保持最低 FPR，同时支持快速插入。它也是唯一遵守内存预算的 dynamic filter。

**图 11：** 随插入进行，Diva 的 FPR 扩展表现最佳，并且是唯一具有稳定内存占用的高效 filter。

**实验 7：Deletion。** 图 12-A 测量随着数据集增长，提供 deletion API 的 filter 的删除性能。每当数据集大小翻倍时，实验发出 50 万次 deletion 并测量 latency。图 12-A 表明，Diva 的 deletion 最快，最多分别比 Memento filter 和 SNARF 快 $2.54\times$ 和 $300\times$。SNARF 的 block 会随插入增大，迫使其重写更大的 block，因此 deletion speed 随插入恶化。Memento filter 也会变慢，因为需要移动的 slot 数量增加。相比之下，Diva 通过拆分 Infix Store 来控制所需的移动和重写量。

**实验 8：End-to-End Query Latency。** 我们将 Diva 和 Memento filter 与 WiredTiger 集成，并测量 end-to-end empty range query latency。SNARF 的 update API 效率较低，不适合 dynamic workload，因此本实验将其排除。图 12-B 在每次数据集大小翻倍时测量 end-to-end query latency。该 workload 中每个 key 都关联一个 504-byte value，因此磁盘上的 key-value pair 大小为 512 byte。我们为 WiredTiger 配置了一个大小为磁盘数据量 1% 的 buffer pool。为公平比较，我们从这部分内存中取出一部分供 range filter 使用。图 12-B 中标记为“Baseline”的曲线表示不使用 filter 的 WiredTiger。

Diva 将 WiredTiger 的 query processing 加速了最多三个数量级。此外，由于 Diva 支持 variable-length query，其 query latency 比 Memento filter 快 $\approx 85\times$。

**图 12：** Diva 在 dynamic workload 下实现了最佳 deletion 和 end-to-end query 性能。

sections.title: 6 CONCLUSION
sections.originalMarkdown: We introduced Diva, the first range filter to simultaneously attain the six range filtering goals. We showed, both theoretically and empirically, that Diva provides support for variable-length queries and keys with excellent FPR, dynamicity, and performance.
sections.translationMarkdown: 我们提出了 Diva，这是第一个同时达成六项 range filtering 目标的 range filter。我们从理论和实验两方面证明，Diva 支持 variable-length query 和 key，同时具有优秀的 FPR、动态性和性能。
